output: Mr. President, dear colleagues, I once again ask for your pardon for my delay.
This year marks the European Year of Intercultural Dialogue.
The involvement of all and the recognition that education, knowledge, and mutual understanding among different cultures are crucial has already become an established fact.
For this reason, numerous initiatives linked to this year have been carried out by the various areas of action and intervention.
In this context, the Erasmus Mundus programme also plays its part, and under this new perspective, the new regulation is already in place.

The Erasmus Mundus programme represents an important contribution to the creation of centers of excellence within the EU, which will, to some extent, help stem the brain drain from outside the European Union, and also plays a significant role in...

in promoting European values among nationals of third countries who come here to study and who find in the old continent an unparalleled cultural and linguistic diversity, serving as an attractive and distinctive pole compared to models existing elsewhere in the world.
However, dialogue and interculturality have suffered greatly in order to survive.

The problems surrounding student visas for Erasmus students in Europe, along with the frequent difficulties in obtaining and renewing them, often result in these students living in a state of semi-clandestinity within the European Union, as they frequently pursue master's degrees while holding tourist visas, sometimes expired.
I consider it of utmost relevance and urgency to find a solution that ensures solidity, transparency, and effectiveness in issuing student visas.
Knowledge of the language that enables cultural awareness and mutual coexistence, beyond strictly academic competence, is also crucial; thus, we can guarantee this premise without reservation.
Finally, a word of thanks to Deputy Dessa Arnez for the way she led the entire process and for the balanced report she produced.

Mr. President, Commissioner, esteemed colleagues, authorization for granting State aid to modernize and renew fishing fleets in the outermost regions should be permitted only until the end of 2009, or should it be extended indefinitely?
The specific characteristics of fishing activities in these regions justify that such aid may be granted beyond 2009 without causing any minimal harm to the marine environment.
State aid for the modernization and renewal of the fleets of the outermost regions for an indefinite period does not mean permanent and eternal aid.
would be immediately halted if the studies and assessments conducted by the Scientific Council and the Regional Advisory Boards indicated such a need.
This is the solution that would best suit the very specific reality of fisheries in the outermost regions.

The same reasons that justify for the fishermen of each RUP a exclusive fishing reserve up to 10 nautical miles also justify a differentiated approach regarding assistance for fleet modernization and renewal.
Fortunately, within the Fisheries Committee, the best solutions were found that can address the specificities of fishing in the outermost regions. Many thanks.

Thank you very much, Mr. President. Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner Almunia, Mr. President of the Eurogroup, I would like to congratulate not only the rapporteurs but also the Commission for its initiative in producing this text, the review of the past 10 years of the Economic and Monetary Union.
It is a strategic document, and this analysis was essential.
The euro, as has been said here today, is an undeniable success.

From Lisbon to Helsinki, from Dublin to Bratislava, the euro has demonstrated resilience and solidity even amid the severe turbulence we have witnessed, and it is clearly important that the boundaries of this club be expanded.
But the times we live in daily put the solidity of the European project to the test in many other dimensions.
The euro, as I said, is a central instrument for the functioning of the financial system.
However, the Commission's own text clearly points to the fundamental conclusion that neither the growth of the real economy nor convergence, whether social or spatial, have accompanied this success.
On the contrary, the Commission clearly concludes that disparities worsened sharply during the decade of the single currency's operation.
This divergence particularly affects regions of my country, especially northern Portugal, but also other regions of member states.

The impacts of the single monetary policy, particularly due to the very high exchange rate applied, have affected more severely those regions most exposed to international competition and the most export-oriented regions.
Today, the crisis resulting from the deregulation of financial markets violently impacts the real economy and further worsens the situation of many citizens and many regions.

These regions—those exposed to international competition, those dependent on small and medium-sized enterprises, and those where access to credit is most critical—are currently suffering from this process and may even face depression.
Mr. Commissioner, Mr. President of the Eurogroup, the success of the euro depends on the trust that European citizens place in you.
The most powerful European countries initiated processes already aimed at revitalizing their national economies.
We have, in particular, the case of initiatives concerning the automotive industry.
But Europe is more than that.
It must be considerably more than a rather coordinated sum of national policies.
The time has come to transform the euro into a central instrument for Europe, supporting not only a strong currency but also a robust real economy, and to ensure the proper functioning of the euro.
of mechanisms that ensure the central objective of achieving social and regional convergence, as this is at the heart, at the core, of the European project.
The debate on the coordination of economic policies in the euro area today has an unquestionably reinforced and undeniable impact and relevance.
But coordinating national policies is not enough.
More is needed.

Having a Lisbon strategy that is a compilation of national initiatives and a stability and growth pact that imposes differentiated constraints on the various countries required to comply with it are limitations to which we must add the limited budget at our disposal.
Crises generate opportunities.
The Monetary Union can only be sustained through a convergence of well-being among the member countries, regardless of their citizens, irrespective of where they were born.
What other way would we have to engage citizens in the single currency?

The Commission must live up to its own diagnosis, which is why we are eagerly awaiting the concrete proposals that the Commission will bring before this plenary session and present to European citizens on 26 November next.

It is essential that, from this crisis, a new phase of the Union be launched in which the consolidation and strength of the financial system are accompanied by genuine prosperity based on cohesion and economic citizenship as well.

Thank you, Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, dear colleagues. This oral question submitted to the European Commission by Gerardo Galiotta on behalf of the Committee on Regional Development of this Parliament is fully justified and relevant.

In fact, it is necessary to clearly state the reasons why the Council is blocking the reform of the Solidarity Fund, given that it is unable to reach a common position enabling the legislative process to proceed.
It is unlikely that the underlying reasons for the Council's position would be discontinued, except perhaps for petty financial considerations.
Does the Council not want a faster and more immediate response to natural disasters that occur?
I do not want the Council to give this kind of famous response to other types of disasters, such as major industrial accidents, terrorist attacks, or serious public health situations.

It is important that these issues receive a clear response, just as it is important to clarify what the European Commission thinks about this situation and whether it intends to take any measures aimed at unblocking the legislative process.
The value of solidarity must also be fully realized in this domain.
the citizens would not understand at all if it were not so.
I wish to say that I wanted to protest against the statement made by Mrs. Luling, which seeks to condition the votes of Members of this House, and such conditioning is nonsensical.

The interpretation you are presenting is one we do not share, and it is in this sense that I would ask my colleagues—those who know what they are dealing with, who are aware that we are defending other interests, namely public health, the interests of citizens, and the interests of countries—that they vote in favor of the report. Thank you very much.
Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, for two months the political and media agenda has been dominated by the financial and economic crisis.
The referendum in Ireland or the difficulties in the Czech Republic regarding the Treaty of Lisbon have almost no coverage in the media.

Don't you think that the Commission's information and communication strategy should now be strengthened by emphasizing the need and urgency of the Lisbon Treaty's entry into force, also as a political response to confront and overcome the crisis we are experiencing?
Madam President-in-Office, Madam Commissioner, dear colleagues, the powers of the world currently have the international financial system in their hands.
They were eager to get their hands on it, and it was high time.
And therefore, perhaps it is time for us to address right now another problem about which we have heard for decades.
For decades we have heard this music of the shameful plundering of resources, how many times—just as now once again in the Congo—over the blood of populations.

I believe it is also time to pursue these companies, to target this real estate trade, to freeze the accounts through which the proceeds of this shameful exploitation of mineral resources—achieved at the cost of people's blood and suffering—are circulated and deposited.
I don't understand why these companies are not listed under the same heading under which we already maintain a blacklist of terrorist organizations.

And I fail to understand why the international community is unable to pursue these businessmen, who in reality are not businessmen at all, but genuine criminals undermining regional and global security.
I urge the French Presidency and the Commission to support an international initiative in this regard. Thank you very much.
We have heard here today on several occasions that a crisis normally generates an opportunity, and this is indeed true.
And if we wish to seize the opportunity that is open to us, the agenda is easy to understand.
First of all, we know, at the level of the European Union, the need we have to introduce mechanisms of transparency, regulation, and supervision in financial markets.
And it is not necessary for Commissioner Macrivi to launch further consultations, nor for the Presidency to conduct and cite additional studies.
more proposals. Just look at the proposals timely made by the socialists, many of which were eventually taken up by this Parliament.
Secondly, internationally, Europe cannot occupy the international arena in a passive manner.
It must be an active agent in building the new international order. It must be the defender of a new architecture that does not leave black holes in the system, such as tax havens are today.
Thirdly, today we have heard the President of the Commission say that there is reluctance in coordinating strategies at the level of the various Member States.
It is true that if we had not heard, from many members of the Commission, for so long, that markets self-regulate, that the state should stay out,
that financial markets do not affect growth or employment, perhaps there would be a more active and dynamic spirit of solidarity at the level of different countries.
But today the proposal before us is merely a first step. If we compare it with the programs of China and the United States, on the other hand, it...
it does not clearly define what the objective is, and the objective must be employment; and thirdly, because a European programme cannot be merely a sum of national agendas.
Today is the time for us to show European citizens that Europe is a network of economic rights that also protects them in times of crisis and in terms of security.
solidarity, growth programs, and support for small and medium-sized enterprises that at this moment are not achieving the results of the measures implemented at the banking level.
The real economy is precisely this, and we are here as Europeans to show citizens that Europe is present here too, and above all, because of them. Many thanks.
Mr. President, dear colleagues, it is with particular satisfaction that I stand before you to present the work carried out jointly by all of us in this initiative report.
For me and for all those who contributed to the progression of this process, we can say that we have done our part.

to all, particularly to the shadow rapporteurs from the various political groups who made great efforts to reach agreements for the future, to the regional development team, always available, especially Miguel Tel Cremades and Elisa da Farra,

to the staff of the DG PSE, to Lila and Petros, and to the European Commission, which, always represented by the many different directorates-general required by such a comprehensive report, closely followed and actively supported the work throughout, making a significant effort to ensure its successful completion.
to my office, and particularly to Joana Benzinho, my most sincere thanks on behalf of a more harmonious and structured development for coastal regions and for tourism across the European Union.
The 27 member states of the European Union collectively have over 89,000 kilometers of coastline.
It presents a variety of very distinctive characteristics that, according to its location, are marked either by cosmopolitanism, if we speak of cities such as Lisbon, Copenhagen, or Stockholm, or

due to the difficulties of being located in peripheral or ultraperipheral regions, facing connectivity issues with major urban centers, or experiencing accelerated desertification contexts, such as the Algarve, Liguria, the Canary Islands, or Madeira.
In one way or another, everyone identifies with the concept of coast and coastal zone and daily experiences the advantages and disadvantages associated with them.
According to available data, by 2010, approximately 75% of humanity will live in coastal areas.
According to the circuits and their interrelations, we define the 50 km in a straight line extending from the coastline into inland areas.
Yes, regions—and not detached maritime strips—within an integrated perspective that these matters so greatly require, and that our ancestors would also endorse.
Here, this entire population will concentrate in search of opportunities and economic synergies, and in many cases, will have only the expectation that these revolve around tourism.
Thus, the evident need for a pragmatic and integrated understanding of tourism's impact on coastal areas seized our minds and prompted us to take action.

In the current context of financial crisis, in which the impact on the real economy becomes increasingly unavoidable, tourism emerges as a sector with immense potential to be severely affected, directly or indirectly.

Regions whose development depends overwhelmingly or entirely on this sector see their activities threatened and face the future with suspicion, particularly because tourism is not currently a competence of the European Union.
However, coordinated measures must be taken, and the spirit in which the Treaty of Lisbon was imbued reflects this same tendency.
However, waiting until the moment the treaty comes into force to begin acting is waiting for the stage when we will weep over spilled milk.
The present political tourism activity and the fragile reality of the regions dependent on it demand from us urgent and effective action.

The fact that these areas are structurally dependent on tourism as a driver of employment, despite much of this employment being seasonal, means that this labor-intensive activity cannot be overlooked in a context of pressure on urban areas and unemployment.

For this very reason, this report, which was already of the utmost importance and urgency when the Committee on Regional Development of this House decided to produce it, must now be regarded from the outset as a priority for the European Commission and the European Council.

Priority, yes, to the numerous initiatives it encompasses, as well as I believe it assumes its explicit support, already expressed by other institutions, alongside the measures taken within the emergency plan.

from the Commission, among which I highlight the review of the Globalisation Adjustment Fund, in which issues related to this sector and the current shock it is experiencing must obviously be addressed.

It is crucial to ensure the development of new sectors of the economy in these coastal areas, guaranteeing their social and environmental sustainability and promoting the effective integration of different sectoral policies, such as the maritime sector, transport, and the...

energy, the cohesion instruments already on the ground, the new quality products policy introduced by the reform of the CAP through the announcement of the Else Cheque report, and the new tourism products in these coastal areas, taking into account their crucial contribution to the European economy.
The implementation of a proper and holistic approach to this policy must become a reality in the near future in the European Union.

And to conclude, Mr. President, only through a clear coordination of these instruments and swift, effective action involving all partners and stakeholders already on the ground can we ensure that the European Union will have a sustainable and future-oriented coastal tourism sector. Thank you very much.
Mr. Commissioner and Madam Minister, e-Justice is an important step towards access to the law, justice, and courts.
I would like to commend the initiative and congratulate the rapporteur and the French Presidency.

e-Justice was one of the projects under discussion, and it falls to Portugal, during its presidency, to lead the development and implementation of the pilot project that will enable all citizens to access services from other Member States in a simpler, cheaper, more comfortable, and language-appropriate manner.
This multilingual portal should be designed to serve citizens and businesses seeking legal assistance and basic legal advice on cross-border legal issues.

E-Justice must be coordinated and managed by a responsible unit, which will also oversee the coordination of contributions from the various Member States and ensure their alignment with this responsible unit.
The Former Justice will serve the citizen and strengthen our democracy.
I welcome the launch of the portal in December 2009. Many thanks.
Mr. President, Mr. Barro, colleagues, I congratulate the work of Carlos Coelho, of the former team Martin Rur, and of the many former team members.
In December 2004, the Council adopted the Regulation establishing standards for the security features and biometric data of passports and travel documents issued by Member States.
Now, we have had the need to take new steps toward combating child abduction and trafficking.
The use of passports by children, in accordance with the principle of one person, one passport, may be the fundamental means of overcoming this difficult and important struggle.

In my country, Portugal, taking digital fingerprints from children starting at the age of six is already a practice with a long-standing tradition, which perhaps is why it does not cause me any objection.
As a defender of the European cause, I believe that, in this matter, harmonization is important.
It reassures me that Member States, such as Portugal, which have long established the minimum age of six years for collecting fingerprints, will not need to amend their national legislation.
I must emphasize that the security of the passports being strengthened today goes beyond the mere existence of the passport itself.

The passport, by itself, represents an increase in security levels, ranging from the submission of required documents for passport issuance, through the collection of biometric data, to verification and matching at border control checkpoints.
This report represents another step toward affirming the rights of the individual and ensuring their safety.
Mr. President, first of all, I would like to welcome the rapporteur Alexander Lambsdorff and the shadow rapporteurs, with particular recognition for Barbara Weiler.
Dear colleagues, I believe it is essential that we move forward toward the integration of national defense markets and even the strategic coordination of their production.

We must effectively advance, with the necessary specifics and precautions, toward applying the fundamental rules of the internal market to defense industries, increasing transparency in the tenders organized and the contracts signed, involving European Union member states.
At the same time, we must help create the conditions that enable European equipment and products to achieve greater competitiveness in global markets.

Commissioner Macrifi. It seems to me important that, through this directive, progress can be ensured in the legal framework governing public contracts in the fields of defense and security, thereby advancing the construction of the market.

internal, while obviously respecting the interests and rights of the Member States, particularly in the field of security, and notably those of arms, ammunition, and security equipment manufacturers, such as my own country.

Mr. President, I would also highlight the provisions that facilitate easier access for small and medium-sized enterprises to this market, particularly through the improvement of subcontracting rules and the prospect of advancing with greater impact and depth within the fabric of the economy.

industrial to create a genuine European market in defense industries, which could also contribute to strengthening research and development not only in defense industries, but across a range of European industries that benefit from advancements in defense industries.
To conclude, I believe one can refer to the obvious connection between defense industries and the Union's foreign policies.
But I would like to refer to this issue by citing a recent intervention by DET, translating the classic Roman proverb: "If you want peace, prepare for war."

The European Union, as it is nearly a peaceful superpower, we refer to these industries as defense industries and not war industries, because we seek peace and not war, and therefore we also need defense industries.
Mr. Vice-President Barro, I agree with you, I agree with you about the dissatisfaction.
In fact, we must be dissatisfied with the overall set of measures, which are extensive.
The truth is that irregular immigration continues to Europe, human trafficking persists, and there are areas where there is a great deal of...
migration, particularly in the Mediterranean, toward Italy. France has advanced a set of important bilateral cooperations, but other Member States have not.
It is necessary that the Commission continue, without losing enthusiasm, along this policy of cooperation and assistance to the Member States.
Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, I will try to present the issues in a very concise manner.
First point: the issue regarding the European Union's special monitoring mechanism for tests, which ended, as has been stated here, on December 31, 2008.
Mrs. Commissioner, timely and routine publication of statistical data on imports, exports, and prices is lacking, in contrast to what occurs in the United States.
Without these data, the European Union is unable to respond to potential unfair practices, and the Commission itself is unable to define its strategy.
Ask for your attention regarding this, in the same way that other colleagues have already done.
Secondly, producing within the European Union increasingly requires compliance with safety standards, social standards, and environmental standards.
The RITS, for example, is a recent initiative that imposes even more constraints upon us.
It is important that the Commission has a clear strategy that is perceivable and measurable, so that imported products meet identical requirements.
In what way are these aspects being properly addressed in the ongoing free trade agreements?
And regarding the Maidin, can the Maidin actually help resolve this issue?
Third point: The European Union has launched an economic recovery plan to combat the current crisis.
What role will commercial policy play in this context?

At this moment, what is the Commission preparing to do when a number of other countries, as is the case with China, begin to increasingly proliferate non-tariff barriers, preventing our European imports from accessing the Chinese market?

What proposal do you have regarding the updating or adjustment of the Globalisation Fund, as well as the available support under the structural funds frameworks, to improve the immediate situation of the European textile industry?
Last question, finally, indeed, will the crisis we are going through finally make the Commission sensitive to the devastating impact on the European economy of an overvalued euro?
How can the Commissioner sensitize, to conclude, her colleagues and the institutions responsible for European monetary policy to the need for rebalancing? Many thanks.
For us, it is clear that we must applaud Obama's decision—the decision to close Guantánamo, to ban torture in illegal interrogations, and to suspend military trials.
Nevertheless, dear colleague, who is no longer present, Portugal and the Portuguese socialist government have no sense of guilt whatsoever.
The Portuguese socialist government did not cooperate with the Bush administration, quite the opposite.

It was precisely now, with the Portuguese administration, with the Portuguese government, and through the initiative of Minister Luís Amado, that the possibility and duty of the European Union and democratic countries to welcome and receive those detained without formal charges at Guantánamo were triggered.

That is truly what matters, that is what should be highlighted as an example to be followed by the other Member States of the European Union. It is crucial that tomorrow the resolution be adopted by all the political parties comprising this Chamber, so that, united, the decision may carry greater weight.
Mr. President, thank you very much to colleague Napolitano for the excellent report.

Fourteen years after Barcelona and five years after the first Euro-Mediterranean Parliamentary Assembly, we now have the Union for the Mediterranean, endowed with economic power, parliamentary authority, civil society engagement, and heads of government collectively making decisions.
All eager to do more and better in a territory where 720 million citizens represent potential for development and peace for the world.
Focusing on them solely from the economic and business perspective is a clear mistake.

Responding to growth crises naturally requires political response, but responding to humanitarian crises, emergency situations, and military crises—such as the one experienced at the end of 2008 and beginning of 2009—is crucial.
Politicians and citizens must provide answers to the world's problems.
The world asked for answers, and months later, we still, as citizens and politicians—particularly on a platform such as the Union for the Mediterranean—linger in failing to respond.
I hope, at the very least, this moment has served to learn and progress, as only those who make mistakes truly learn; however, advancing the speed of response is absolutely crucial.
The interest in adopting this proposed directive essentially lies in its potential to stimulate local economic activity and support social initiatives.

The application of a reduced rate is of great importance in the housing sector, both in construction and renovation, enabling Member States that adopt it to lower the associated costs for the end user.

The provision of local services, particularly by small enterprises, may also benefit from this reduction in the VAT rate, thereby retaining and generating more and better employment by encouraging the consumption of goods and services, thus stimulating the economy itself.
The restoration activity, essential for the proper functioning of local economies and the very growth of tourism, could gain significantly at the local level through this reduction in VAT taxation.
These services, dear colleagues, should not circulate within the European internal market, which will help reduce the reserves already observed by some Member States in the Council's plan.
Therefore, I cannot agree with extending this reduced rate to equipment, regardless of the argument.

The prudent manner in which this proposal should be handled in the European Parliament must not provide an excuse for governments that have certain reservations regarding the European Commission's proposal to actually exercise the veto power still granted to them under European legislation in fiscal matters.
And thus here we must prudently reject changes that lack substantial meaning, as they only hinder the acceptance of the text.
As already mentioned here, not only is the perfect the enemy of the good, but he who wants everything loses everything.
This proposed directive is important not only for strengthening local economies, but also for aligning with the ambitions repeatedly expressed by various Member States.
We therefore support the Commission's proposal as it was presented. Indeed, this was the intent of the vote, as I understand it, of the Committee on the Internal Market and Consumer Protection.

Health to the work of the rapporteurs and an appeal for approval of this proposal exactly as it came from the Commission and was voted on by the Parliamentary Committee on the Internal Market and Consumer Protection. Thank you very much.
The word to disagree with my colleague Edith Estrela, and indeed that was precisely what motivated me to speak. When she caricatured the intentions of the Czech Presidency.
No one wants to send women home. The problem is that there are women who want to or would like to stay at home.

There are even associations that seek to defend them—women who are ignored, dismissed, discriminated against, simply because they wish to dedicate themselves to their families, and yet we do not listen to them.
And this is a matter of freedom, a matter of the freedom of choice that the left so often invokes, yet forgets here.
It is a matter of respecting or not the freedom of orientation of couples.
It is about enabling those who wish to do so to achieve a better quality of life, better fatherhood, and better motherhood.
That is what our society also needs to do.
We won't get there by resolving people's issues of birth rates and happiness through prejudice.
We have resolved it through policies tailored to reality and people's spontaneous desires.
We are not going there with an obsession about the State and the market.
Let us now take a fair look at the family.
Commissioner, colleagues, the difficult situation at Quimonda has been exacerbated by the onset of the financial and economic crisis.
The Portuguese government has done everything it considers appropriate and useful to help resolve the situation.
And we would also like the Bavarian Government to know how to act with great sense of responsibility in this area.
Saving the Munich facilities will have a decisive impact on the Vila do Conde and Dresden units.

The Portuguese minister himself, Manuel Pinho, stated the strong interest in making this company, so important for Portugal and for Europe, viable, noting that Quimonda could become a globally competitive enterprise.
Mr. President, the Government of Portugal will continue its efforts toward a solution that ensures the company's viability.
We hope that the German federal and state governments will effectively commit themselves to resolving this issue.
As for me, I would like to re-emphasize once again the strategic importance of maintaining this type of industry within European territory.
that no national or state executive should make the mistake of allowing this company to close facilities and eliminate jobs within the territory of the Union.

Dear colleagues, we understand the need to assist in the restructuring of major groups in the automotive industry, but the funds of the Union and the resources of the European Commission cannot be exhausted by such aid.

Dear Commissioner, we appeal to Commissioner Vladimir Spidla, to whom we are bound by ties of collaboration and political solidarity, and to President Barroso, who will never forget that he is our compatriot and also of a significant portion of Quimonda's workers, urging the Commission to commit itself to supporting Quimonda, overcoming any potential communication shortcomings.

We should emphasize, before concluding, that Portuguese President Cavaco Silva recently stated in Germany that there was new hope regarding Quimonda. Furthermore, as already noted by colleagues, the significance of the economic sector in which Quimonda operates must also be highlighted.
We note that, as soon as the Portuguese government expresses its willingness to support Quimonda with whatever may be necessary, taking into account the country's economic scale.
Ladies and gentlemen, let us help save Quimonda? Time is of the essence.

Mr. President, Commissioner, we have been discussing the Lisbon Strategy for years, and the times we are living in may not be the most suitable for talking about the Lisbon Strategy, but we undoubtedly need a strategy.
in a strategy that responds to the difficulties and challenges that the current crisis presents to us.
And that is also what we expect from the Commission.

It is essential that the Commission does not distance itself from this issue and, instead, has the capacity to coordinate actions with the governments of Portugal and the governments of Germany and the German lenders.

It is essential to bear in mind that this is an industry, as already stated here, of importance to Europe due to its quality and value, the research it carries out, and the environmental quality associated with it as well, and it is essential that the Commission does not distance itself.
I endorse the invitation made by my colleague Pineda for the Commissioner and the Commission to visit Portugal.

It is essential that the Commission bear in mind that, at this juncture, Europeans are looking to Europe and expect responses from European authorities. They need to feel that European authorities are close at hand.

European citizens do not understand a Europe that washes its hands of problems. They prefer a Europe that has the courage to roll up its sleeves and get to work, helping them overcome their difficulties. Thank you very much.

Mr. President, thank you very much. I join in extending my tribute to Jamonet, but I requested the floor to congratulate the President on the statement he made regarding the European Day of Remembrance for the Victims of Terrorism.
It was through my proposal that, five years ago, we adopted this approach, which the Council formally endorsed during a meeting on March 25, following the tragic attacks in Madrid.
but my intervention was an appeal.

The Parliament has always remained faithful to commemorating this date; however, unfortunately, this date has still not received, across the European institutions and the Member States, the recognition it deserves.
I believe that commemorating this date is one of the most important tools we have to honor the victims, as the Mr. President did, but also to raise the level of public awareness.
I know there are some celebrations in Madrid today, but nothing more.
And therefore, my appeal to the Commission and the Czech Presidency is that all Member States solemnly observe this date in the future. Thank you very much.
and my dear colleagues, the crisis is deeper than we expected, and unemployment will be greater than we foresaw.
There are strong reasons to believe the anticipated European stimulus is insufficient, but it is already clear at this stage that it is taking far too long to reach citizens.
The Parliament's position is strong and clear. It has been so, and I hope it will continue to be.
Our objective is to maintain employment and create new jobs, with cohesion and solidarity, both territorial and social.
And at this moment of crisis, citizens cannot accept an Europe that fails to respond to their needs, an Europe that is powerless in the face of the challenges they are experiencing.
What is the Parliament going to request from the Commission?

It naturally requests, through these reports, that you coordinate national interventions. It asks you to use all available means at your disposal to act, and provides you with all necessary conditions as a budgetary entity to enable this to happen.

It urges him to launch a clear European initiative on employment and tells him that it is important to have a timetable-driven agenda to implement measures regulating the financial market, as well as to ensure credit reaches the real economy.
But what is the Council waiting for?
The Council is urged to rediscover, above all, the political will that lies at the heart of the European project's construction.
The European Union is competition, but it is equally cohesion and solidarity.
And there cannot be a single market without this guarantee, solidarity, and cohesion.
That is precisely why we all, at the European level, delegated the national competences we previously held before joining this project. Thank you very much.

Madam President, Madam Commissioner, anyone visiting sub-Saharan Africa readily recognizes in the majority of countries the immense weakness of their healthcare systems and the extremely negative impact this weakness has on the lives and health of the populations these services should serve.
The indicators continually disseminated internationally continue to confirm this.
And to that extent, it is absolutely disturbing to consider that simple gestures and practices—neither particularly elaborate nor especially costly—might be sufficient to save many lives.

European financial support may be crucial in this regard, and we must never lose sight of how cooperation in the field of health is truly strategic, directly addressing not only one of the Millennium Development Goals but also many of them.

Indeed, the Court of Auditors considered that, to quote, community funding for the health sector has not increased since 2000 in proportion to its overall aid for development, despite the commitments made by the Commission regarding the Millennium Development Goals and the public health crisis in sub-Saharan Africa.
Do that.

And acknowledged, once again quoting the Commission, it made a significant contribution, through its funding, toward establishing the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria, but did not give the same attention to strengthening health systems, which was expected to be one of its priorities.
End, citation.

According to the Court, this occurred, I quote again, because the Commission lacked sufficient experience in health matters to ensure that funding provided by it to this sector was being used in the most effective manner.
End it.
This therefore constitutes a significant challenge directly issued by the Court of Auditors to the European Commission, which I support.
On our part, I wish to reiterate this same challenge, now grounded in the objectivity of these data and this assessment.
Health services are already part of our development assistance priorities, but they should increasingly be included, deserving greater funding.

Optimizing the way aid is delivered, while balancing the seemingly opposing needs of coordination in its management and proximity to beneficiary populations, is providing a service that can save many lives.
The European Commission cannot but respond positively to this timely call, and it is in this sense that I urge it.
Colleague Bowies, not long ago, delivered a moving intervention here and managed to give faces—human faces—to the cold, dry figures presented by the Court of Auditors.
The challenge for us, Madam Commissioner, is to enable our cooperation to instill in these very same faces a look of joy and hope.
And for this, Madam Commissioner, it is essential that the figures in the field of health within our cooperation be different.
Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, dear colleagues, once again, for sad reasons, we are discussing here the situation in Guinea-Bissau, which is truly distressing.
It is a country that has experienced chronic instability for many years, yet taught a path toward democracy in the early 1990s.

Nothing went well; there was a coup d'état, a minor civil war, and a country living in great political and military instability, with deep-seated tensions and rivalries. And now, most recently, there is also the increasingly troubling and unmistakable presence—evident to all observers—of narcotics trafficking interests.
We strongly condemn these attacks, the bombing assassination of Chief of Staff Tagume Naoué, and also the particularly barbaric, to say nothing of savage, murder of President Nineviera.
Regardless of their past, we express our solidarity with their families, with the people of Guinea-Bissau, and we regret, and severely condemn, these attacks.
We wish for a return to normality, and the lesson I draw—and would like to emphasize in this resolution—is that impunity is not the solution.
In the past, regarding the murder of Asuman Mané and the journalist Verissim Siabra, how did you turn a blind eye to the fact that the perpetrators were not found and brought to justice?
evident, he sought new sources.
And therefore, we must make clear to the State of Guinea-Bissau that those responsible must be identified, the guilty must be brought to justice, and we must provide all necessary assistance.

I would also like, to conclude, to draw attention to the concern we have regarding the presence of drug trafficking throughout the region, the risk it poses to the European Union as well, and its striking and undeniable presence in Guinea-Bissau.
I would also like to appeal that, in this context, we consider the relationship with Cape Verde,

We have established a special partnership with Cape Verde. Cape Verde maintains very close ties with Guinea, possesses extensive knowledge, but is also highly vulnerable—and this is essential for our own European security as well.
Therefore, intensifying the special partnership with Cape Verde is also very important in this context. Many thanks.
Madam President, esteemed colleagues, I would first like to thank everyone for the work accomplished.

The three lines outlined in the Green Paper—concentration, interconnection, and cooperation—can provide solutions to some obstacles hindering the harmonious development of the Union, particularly the negative effects associated with...

concentration of economic activity, inequalities in access to markets and services resulting from distance, and divisions imposed both by borders between member states—particularly the less advantaged ones—and between regions.
We should therefore promote the improvement of synergies among these policies through effective methods for assessing their territorial impact.

For this reason, from the outset I have advocated defining supplementary qualitative indicators aimed at improving the design and implementation of corresponding field policies, taking into account the different territorial specificities.
For now, GDP continues to be the sole criterion for eligibility to receive support from structural funds.

However, this definition of supplementary indicators and the implementation of territorial assessments should not lead to additional bureaucracy or delays, but rather to simplification in the application of new policies and support actions for territorial cohesion.
The fifth interim report, and to conclude, Mr. President, makes a specific reference to the transition regions situated between the convergence regions and the competitiveness and employment regions.
It is essential to note that these regions need a clearer status, with greater security and stability for their development. Thank you very much.

Madam Commissioner, esteemed colleagues, in the context of this major debate on territorial cohesion and the future of economic and social cohesion policy, I could not fail to mention the very specific issues facing the outermost regions.

regions that, due to the combination and persistence of a series of geographical factors, reveal a high level of economic and social vulnerability, particularly felt in the context of a severe international crisis such as the one we are currently experiencing.
I would therefore like to appeal to the European Commission, and in particular to Commissioner Hübner, to pay particular attention to the effects of the current crisis on the outermost regions.

An assessment of these effects in each of the outermost regions, particularly in tourism, construction, and rising unemployment, would be important in enabling a specific European response to these regions.

Therefore, I challenge the European Commission to develop a European response to the crisis for the overseas territories that goes beyond the measures already announced within the framework of the economic and social cohesion policy for the majority of European regions.
a specific European response to the crisis for the outermost regions.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, in response to the global financial crisis, the European Economic Recovery Plan states that cohesion policy significantly contributes to public investment by Member States and regions, and should serve as a means of recovering from the current crisis.
It specifically suggests the adoption of actions in priority areas of Lisbon's strategy, in order to achieve growth and employment.
All activated instruments aim to achieve this purpose and accelerate the attainment of results.

In this regard, expanding the scope of the European Globalisation Adjustment Fund and accelerating the European Social Fund particularly entails the responsibility of addressing the various situations of social and economic emergency that require support.
I am certain, today as in the past, that your action will be all the more effective the more complementary your actions and domains are.

However, since no new funds or additional field-level initiatives have been foreseen, it is particularly important that the European Social Fund be fully utilized in addressing unemployment and the rapidly increasing pressure of competitiveness in the European economy, resulting from the current financial crisis and economic slowdown.

I would like to emphasize that the Committee on Regional Policy has repeatedly identified simplification as essential for improving the management and implementation of structural funds, as the European Commission is now proposing.

Urgency was requested of us in this matter; however, we took this into account in adopting this package of regulations. And this institution has never shied away from assuming its political responsibilities.

Therefore, despite the many serious considerations raised by the particular circumstances of the moment, and in the interest of ensuring the procedural speed and the actual benefit for citizens that the proposal aims to achieve, we refrain, for now, from presenting new proposals and amendments.
However, we caution against the need to initiate, from now on, an evaluation process of this fund with a view to a supplementary review to be carried out as soon as possible. Thank you very much.
Madam President, the crisis, among other aspects, has highlighted the significant imbalances between the countries in the eurozone, perhaps the main...

conclusion of the report on the Economic and Monetary Union ten years ago, but above all, the extreme vulnerability of countries that, although belonging to the European Union, do not belong to the eurozone.

Hence, the reinforcement of the resources available at the European level to support balance of payments crises is absolutely essential, and hence we welcome the increase from 12 to 25 and then to 50 million euros of necessary resources.
However, more important is to address the root causes of the problems, rather than merely their most apparent manifestations.
Today, the enlarged Europe and the euro area must reinterpret the meaning of European solidarity and the objectives of cohesion and genuine convergence.
It is necessary to assess, in this light, the capacity of European horizontal policies, but it is also essential to revisit new financing instruments, such as eurobonds.
Financial assistance to the balance of payments is necessary, but it is far from being sufficient to ensure the sustainability of the European project and the single European currency.
Mr. Chairman of the Commission, Mr. President in Waiting, we are facing an unprecedented crisis.

It was indeed not the right time to discuss internally how we should organize ourselves. Therefore, my wish is that the Treaty of Lisbon be rapidly ratified, so that we may face the global stage united and solidarity as Europeans.

Regarding the economic crisis, in the report I authored, which was overwhelmingly approved by this Parliament, it was acknowledged that the recovery plan subsequently presented by the Commission was insufficient.
Today we are certain that it is not enough.
A crisis that began in the financial sector spreads to the real economy, then to employment, and subsequently becomes social.
It is insufficient and out of focus.

Every crisis holds an opportunity. This is the opportunity for the Commission to address the genuine concerns of Europeans through serious financial regulation, and not through the slightest fragment of regulation that may be offered.

possible, as it is currently underway. Not made up of national and individualistic plans, but of a genuine European initiative. Not through scattered means, but by focusing on the real challenge: employment.

In every crisis lies an opportunity. This is the opportunity for the Commission and Europe to address the genuine concerns of European citizens. And I hope this will be achieved through a new approach to the recovery plan. Thank you very much.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, I would like to express, not only on behalf of the PPE, as has already been done by many Members, but particularly on behalf of the Portuguese PPE Members, our great satisfaction with the outcome of the referendum in Ireland.
We believe this clearly strengthens the European Union's development and return prospects, creating conditions for a new phase.

Naturally, we wished to congratulate all those who contributed to the Treaty of Lisbon, in particular the Commission and the Vice-President Commissioner present here, as well as, naturally, the Portuguese Presidency of the European Union.

played a very important role here, and therefore we wish to say with pleasure that we have observed, despite all these vicissitudes and still maintaining anticipation regarding the position of the Czech Republic, that the Portuguese will gladly see the name of their capital linked

a decisive step forward in the evolution of the European Union, and I would like to say that, from this perspective, we can also count on another banner, which will also be a constant banner of Portuguese Members of the European People's Party, namely the banner of the National Parliaments.
We believe the Treaty of Lisbon represents a decisive step toward incorporating the presence of National Parliaments into European democracy.

And therefore we believe that this dimension, which is the most important aspect of democracy, namely the representative dimension, and not, as we sometimes hear in this Chamber, the referendary dimension, that which is...

The fundamental and essential element of democracy is representation, not direct voting; likewise, the Treaty of Lisbon represents a significant step toward increasing the participation of national representative democracies in the European process.
That is why we are very pleased with this decision by the Irish people.

Mr. President, Madam Minister, Madam Commissioner, dear colleagues. A brief intervention to welcome the historic and broad outcome of the referendum in Ireland and to highlight the decisive role played by Fine Gael and its leader, and incoming Prime Minister of Ireland, Enda Kenny, in taking the lead in this process.
a process in which it became very clear the need to bring the European Union much closer to its citizens.
We are them, we are all of us, the primary recipients of all the Union's actions.
It is essential that everyone be aware of the impact and benefits that Europe has in our daily lives.
Europe is our living space.
What is good for Europe is good for each Member State, and good for its citizens.
It is undoubtedly the case with the Treaty of Lisbon.
With this treaty, it becomes much clearer that one can simultaneously be a proud and committed national citizen and a staunch advocate of the European project.

And the European Union, having concluded a debate on its organization and functioning that lasted far too long, may now focus much more effectively—and with far more powerful tools—on the genuine challenges facing Europeans: competitiveness, growth, and employment.
I conclude, Mr. President, Madam Minister, with a proposal intended for future generations.

Given the lack of information regarding the values, competences, objectives, and mode of operation of the European Union, the European Parliament should propose, at the end of compulsory schooling in each Member State, a new mandatory European studies discipline.
In an objective, truthful, and concrete manner, European youth will truly come to understand our principles, who we are, what we do, and where we want to go.

It is through the knowledge of these young people that they will come to understand throughout their lives the potential and immense value of Europe's identity—this unique project of voluntary sharing of sovereignty on a continental scale.

Thank you, Mr. President. Mr. President of the Commission, I begin by congratulating you on this first debate, on what this new instrument of political oversight signifies for the advancement and development of parliamentary democracy in Europe.
It is the European citizens, through their representatives, who stand to benefit.

Taking into account the most recent developments—the Irish referendum, Poland's ratification, and the latest statements by the President of the Czech Republic—I would like to ask for your assessment of the process leading to the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon.
There were proceedings carried out by the Chairperson of the Commission
and what is your forecast regarding a possible date of entry into force of the Treaty, and on the other hand, whether at this stage and based on this forecast, the Commission

having already carried out some transitional procedures from the Treaty of Nice to the Treaty of Lisbon, or whether one is still in a phase, so to speak, of anticipation, thus a phase of waiting to see.

Mr. President of the Commission, having heard this, I would like to ask the following: At this stage, when everyone is awaiting the formation of the Commission, what is, in fact, the President's outlook?
whether we will now have a commission in office until that final moment of ratification by the Czech Republic, or whether they will proceed with the appointment procedures for the new commissioners.

Thank you, Mr. President. It is essential that the primary objective of establishing the European External Action Service materializes the efforts of the European institutions, ensuring the creation of an effective service capable of coordinating, formulating, and implementing a European response to today's international challenges.
It must also represent more than the sum of its parts. It must be an added value relative to the current efforts of the Member States and the European Union.
In this sense, it seems essential to me that what defines the specificity of the Union should be at the heart of this new reality. And that specificity is the community method.
That is why I fully support the Brok report, which advocates for and safeguards a close coordination between the Commission and the future Service.

With this in mind, I would like to highlight two aspects. The importance of close coordination between the President of the Commission and the High Representative, also Vice-President of the Commission, to ensure effectiveness and proper functioning.
Lastly, the need for an alliance between Parliament and the Commission to counter the impending intergovernmental drift, which could jeopardize the effective operation of the Service.
The Parliament must therefore remain vigilant and ensure that the European External Action Service becomes a center of excellence representative of the best expertise in foreign policy.

Thank you, Mr. President. Thank you, Commissioner. First of all, I would like to emphasize the importance of this issue raised by Member of Parliament Golarensky, as it is a matter of the most fundamental justice.

We all fully understand that in a situation of an H1N1 pandemic, it will be the countries in the South that will suffer the most from this problem and from the consequences of the virus's spread, and not the countries in the North.

And every year we passively witness the death of millions of citizens due to pandemics that have already been eradicated or are treatable in Northern countries. An extensive list: malaria, dengue, yellow fever, tuberculosis.
We have no limits for this list, and we passively witness this problem.

We effectively use countries in the Global South as free suppliers of samples from new virus strains, enabling us to improve treatment in Northern countries, yet we remain without an effective response to these issues. Therefore, regarding the questions raised, I would simply like to add a few more.
We genuinely want to address this issue either as a public health problem or as a business.
Because if it is a public health issue, we must ensure equal access to treatment in all regions of the world, and for this, efforts must be more effective.
On the other hand, I would like to ask—hopefully not the case—if we are actually waiting for European vaccine stocks to become so oversupplied after all.

that the secondary effects prove so negative on this level, then indeed we would have an act of generosity, and suddenly we would begin sending the necessary vaccines to countries in the Global South.

And finally, I would simply like to ask whether the European Union, whether we Europeans, or the Commission feel comfortable with a system or model in which Northern countries address pandemics through a preventive approach, while Southern countries deal with them only through palliative measures or merely with leftovers.

Thank you, Mr. President. First of all, I wish to express my full support for the issue raised by Mr. Royal, and particularly to highlight the underlying political dimension of this matter, which is precisely related to the joint programming of research.

What occurred regarding the joint programming on neurodegenerative diseases, and in particular Alzheimer's disease, was that, during the process, the rules changed: we no longer had a report, but instead a resolution, thereby eliminating our capacity for co-decision-making in this matter; we ceased to be Members of Parliament and became consultants.
and therefore, I would like to know whether at least what we have recommended will be taken into account or not.
The programming of research activities, in whatever field, is a political choice, not a technical one. In this sense, the role of Parliament should be recognized and strengthened.
It seems to me that defining priorities that are political means that these priorities must be subject to scrutiny, must be transparent, and must be democratic.
What occurred with the process regarding the joint decision and joint planning of the Alzheimer's research is a process that must not be repeated.

Please repeat that they should notify us in good time. I therefore hope that the last thing that could happen to us is that the decisions and recommendations submitted by Parliament on this matter are not taken into account.
In the past month, the regions of Europe were particularly in the spotlight.
The European Week dedicated to regions and cities reinforced the role of regions as a fundamental component of the European response to global challenges.
Thus they concluded, leaving no room for doubt, President Durão Barroso, Commissioner Samezki, and the hundreds of debates that took place.

Even at the most specific level, the holding of the important meeting of the Conference of Presidents of the European Overseas Regions, which for the first time included participation by members of the European Parliament, represents a milestone in regional policy in support of European integration.
Global challenges and European responses present a great opportunity for a new strategy for the RUP.

These regions, such as the Azores, despite the persistent constraints that everyone is accustomed to hearing about, possess a set of potentialities that offer clear opportunities to advance our progress and provide Europe with a distinct advantage in new, essential domains for addressing global challenges.

It therefore demands that the President of the Commission clearly put an end to the palace coup that the Commission's non-paper indicates was being prepared, while we, as members of Parliament and European citizens, were advocating for the ratification of the Treaty of Lisbon and its importance in safeguarding the cohesion of the European territory.

First of all, I would like to greet the rapporteurs, particularly Mr. Martin and also Mr. Corbett, and say that, as a member of the PPE, I essentially identify with the amendments proposed to the Rules of Procedure.
I would essentially like to emphasize the point that I consider important here and that has not yet been raised tonight, namely the issue of National Parliaments.

The democratic strengthening of the European Parliament undoubtedly stems from this Rules of Procedure and the Treaty of Lisbon, but European democracy also arises from its connection with National Parliaments.
And what this Regulation, this proposal, is now doing is precisely creating these two democratic legitimacies, strengthening democracy.

On the one hand, democracy resulting from the instruments of the European Parliament, its control mechanisms, and its legislative capacity; on the other hand, democratic cooperation among National Parliaments through various instruments.
And thus, here I am, so to speak, fully in tune with the proposals made.
I do not agree with colleague Duff when he suggests that we should postpone the introduction of mechanisms for engaging with National Parliaments.
I believe we can do this immediately, in light of the Treaty of Lisbon, and that this is very important.
I would also like to say that I support the proposal by my colleagues Sayer and Brock regarding representation in the COSAC, and finally, that I am.
Fully aligned with the observations made here by colleague Brock and other colleagues regarding the primacy of the Parliament and its members over the Parliament's services.
Thank you very much, Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues. I congratulate the rapporteur colleague for the work carried out in this report and for her intervention a short while ago.

This new microfinance instrument will enable the granting of microcredit to small businesses and to individuals who have lost their jobs and wish to create their own business and their own employment opportunity.
This is particularly important at a time when an economic crisis is expected to result in the loss of three and a half million jobs across the European Union alone.

As a consequence of the economic slowdown, banks have ceased granting loans for job creation and business development, and access to credit has become more difficult at a time when it should be more accessible.

Indeed, this new form of microfinancing will counteract the current trend of restricted access to credit, facilitating the acquisition of funds necessary for launching new businesses and creating new jobs.
The European Commission's proposal aims to reallocate 100 million euros from the Progress Programme budget to this financing instrument.
We cannot agree with this proposal.
The financial and economic crisis is equally a social crisis.
Thus, the reallocation of resources from Progress, which targets the most vulnerable groups, is not entirely the most appropriate solution.
For this reason, support is given to the creation of a separate budgetary heading to finance this instrument, as well as an increase in the allocation to 150 million euros.

We also agree with the need to clarify in the legislation itself that the target group consists of vulnerable populations who face difficulties in entering or re-entering the labor market and who are at risk of social exclusion.
Thus, references to specific groups should be omitted.
Finally, we emphasize that it is essential for individuals to have financial support as well as appropriate training. Thank you very much.

Thank you very much, Mr. President. I would like to begin by congratulating the Swedish Presidency and, in particular, Prime Minister Reinfeldt, both on behalf of the PPE and on behalf of the Portuguese Delegation of the PPE.
Our assessment of the Swedish Presidency is one of near-total success, essentially across four fundamental areas.
The first in the institutional sphere, the Swedish Presidency's contribution to the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon, and to the highly successful election of the President of the Commission.
in particular, all issues related to ratification associated with ratification, was extremely professional and met the standards expected, best practices of the European Union.
Naturally, for a country like Portugal, which played a decisive role in the approval of the Treaty of Lisbon, this contribution from the Swedish Presidency is invaluable.
A second point is the climate agenda, where, naturally, the Commission's work was also extremely important.
We should say that, from my perspective and that of many PPE members of parliament, it is in the field of climate change that the European Union has achieved its greatest success.
It is truly at the forefront of global combat, and this is due to the efforts of both the Swedish Presidency and, in particular, the Commission's Presidency.
considers this balance very positive.
A third point concerns financial regulation, which, especially with this most recent Council, has taken a step we consider decisive and which could have a significant impact on overcoming the crisis.
And therefore, I would also like to congratulate you on having achieved an agreement in this area.
And finally, I would like to say a few words about an area that personally means a great deal to me, namely the Stockholm Programme, and thus the area of security, justice, and freedom.

I believe that having followed both the Tempere process and, in particular, the Hague process afterwards, I consider this Stockholm programme absolutely essential, and I would like to congratulate the Swedish presidency and Prime Minister Reinfeldt on this.

Thank you, Mr. President. As is well known, the recognition of professional qualifications has been consolidated into a single legislative act comprising 15 directives, which has led to various practical problems in implementation across the different Member States.

The difficulties are well known, and the Commission should identify the critical points and conduct an analysis of the problematic areas arising in the implementation of this important instrument of the internal market.
The protectionism that manifests itself at various levels constitutes an obstacle not only to the free movement of people but also to the internal market and the internal market.
We should strive to identify the ambiguous points so that the directive's effectiveness is not distorted by suspicions.
The Union is not asked to do what Member States should do themselves, but there is a need for an effort to facilitate, which helps to overcome professional corporatism.
An improvement in the application of this directive should involve the dissemination of qualification recognition through the Solvit network and the EURES network, by encouraging...
common platforms, implementation of effective contact points, and, no less importantly, should ensure the alignment of this directive with the Services Directive. Thank you.

Thank you very much, Mr. President, Commissioner, Ladies and Gentlemen Members of Parliament. It is urgent that we initiate a debate on the community budget and on how, in the future, we can contribute to its sustainability and transparency.

The post-2013 financial framework should be built on solidarity and territorial cohesion, values crucial for regions such as Madeira, which face persistent challenges and therefore require continuous support.
I would like to share with you some ideas that I believe should always be present when reflecting on and designing cohesion policy.

The Commission has repeatedly highlighted, on various occasions, certain aspects that I consider essential, such as the need for greater flexibility, simplicity, efficiency, and a stronger focus on results in the design and implementation of this policy.
However, there are issues that raise some concern, particularly when considering the needs of the overseas peripheral regions.
In this matter, allow me three brief remarks. First, the current approach based on regions should prevail over alternative guidelines, such as sectors of added value.

Such a reorientation could jeopardize Objective 2, which currently benefits approximately two-thirds of European regions, and may potentially be counterproductive in regions with distinct economic profiles and unique competitive advantages. Secondly, it seems to me that maintaining the proximity criterion in the cohesion policy is vital.

It should continue to prioritize the allocation and management of structural funds from a regional perspective, rather than a national one, or even a centralized approach at the European level. Third and finally, but no less importantly, I would like to recall and emphasize Article 349.
349 The Treaty of Lisbon provides special treatment for the overseas regions regarding access to structural funds, precisely because of their economic and social situation.
aggravated by conditions and unique, enduring characteristics, which manifest in such persistent difficulties and therefore require the requested permanent assistance. Many thanks.
Thank you, Mr. President. In light of the urgency, what we witnessed in Copenhagen was several steps backward.

It is true that Copenhagen mobilized more heads of state than other summits, but it also created greater divisions. As a result, we ended up with a situation of each for oneself, where each state can set its own targets and, moreover, under a voluntary regime.
It should be noted that we have a target of two degrees Celsius or financing targets without any clarification, which also cannot serve as consolation.
And therefore, what I believe is that we must act in terms of the future. To act in terms of the future is to stop moving from rhetoric to action.
The European Union has always wanted to be a leader, and has always stated so.
but when we arrived in Copenhagen, he ultimately hesitated, failing to define or defend the goals he had here declared to be his own, goals he would not implement.

And therefore, I ask whether, in the future, we will be more just, whether we will continue to allow the poorest to bear the heaviest burden, at the expense of this failure and of decisions made by some over others.
Thank you very much, Mr. President. I would like to draw attention here to what is currently happening in Portugal, where it is evident that the media is
brought to light a government plan to control newspapers, television stations, and radio stations, which undermines freedom of expression.
This week as well, a well-known journalist, Mário Crespo, had one of his columns censored, also due to a suggestion or apparent suggestion from the Prime Minister.

In light of this, Prime Minister José Sócrates must provide substantial explanations to the nation, must clarify that he is not suppressing, restricting, or censoring freedom of expression in Portugal.

As things stand, Portugal is no longer a State of Law, but a State of Formal Law, where the Prime Minister confines himself to mere formalities, procedures, and formalism, and refuses to provide substantial explanations.
For Portugal, we want a material rule of law state.

Thank you very much, Mr. President. The Green Paper on the Reform of the Common Fisheries Policy, whose rapporteur is my colleague from the Azores, Maria do Séu Patrão Neves, presents a unique opportunity for debate on a sector that plays a crucial role in balancing the local development of the outermost regions.
The Madera closely follows the ongoing discussion and considers it essential to establish a distinction between artisanal and industrial fleets, which should be subject to different fishing regimes.
It is essential to uphold a principle of positive discrimination by restricting access to territorial waters within the exclusive economic zone to local artisanal fleets only.
In Madeira, it is a priority to support the modernization of vessels, as well as management practices that ensure the long-term conservation of a broad range of species.
Only in this way will we be able to avoid unsustainable situations for economic, environmental, and social balance.
Finally, we will strive for fisheries in the outermost regions to have permanent support, not temporary measures.
This report on the FEDER aims to broaden the eligibility of interventions in the housing sector to benefit marginalized communities in our Member States.
To date, this support applied only to marginalized communities residing in the urban areas of the new Member States.
That is, since the vast majority of these communities live in rural areas and shelters, they could not benefit from support aimed at replacing substandard housing.
Ultimately, this is about filling a gap and providing Member States and their regions with greater possibilities in the policy of mitigating the specific difficulties faced by these communities.
Health also entails equal treatment for all member states, as marginalized communities with needs for support and integration deserve our concern, regardless of the member state to which they belong.

By adopting this important report at first reading in the fight against poverty and for human dignity, its swift implementation within the framework of the economic recovery plan is also achieved, providing a prompt response to the crisis affecting us.

Thank you very much, Mr. President, Commissioner. Let me begin by highlighting the role of the rapporteur, Mr. Van Nistelrooy, for his efforts in seeking consensus on this matter, as well as for the willingness he has consistently shown to incorporate other contributions into his report.
Tomorrow we will vote on amendments to the FEDER Regulation that assume particular importance for the so-called old member states and also for Portugal.
These changes will allow extending the use of the FEDER in the housing sector to benefit marginalized communities, as currently this fund can only be allocated to urban development operations.

The amendments I proposed together with other colleagues, and which were approved by a large majority in the Committee on Regional Development, will enable not only the new Member States but also the former Member States—just as originally intended by the proposal—to also benefit from this financing opportunity.

With this, I sought to prevent the establishment of a precedent that I consider dangerous and that would exclude all the former Member States from this and likely from other additional uses of community assistance.
which would make no sense, since housing-related problems, particularly among marginalized communities, exist both in new and old member states.

I take this opportunity to reiterate that the length of time since accession to the European Union should not be a criterion for the allocation of structural funds, and that this criterion should be abandoned from the outset.

The negotiation of the post-2013 cohesion policy should be based on solidarity, aiming at territorial cohesion, and should be oriented toward rewarding, rather than punishing, regions that have demonstrated exemplary use of community funding.

At this moment, I would essentially like to emphasize to the Commission and to the President of the Commission the way in which they have worked with this Parliament throughout these months of office.

There is no body in the world with executive and legislative initiative powers that is subject to the presentation by its President of a program before Parliament, followed by hearings with all the groups.

Members of parliament, by an absolute and decisive majority, to bring here all the commissioners to be heard one by one, each with their own programme, over three to four hours of direct questioning, with questions and answers.
There is someone who has agreed to negotiate with Parliament a framework agreement, in which they have first agreed to justify and expand Parliament's powers within the legislative initiative.

that accepted the principle of full access to information, whether in legislative and political action or in international negotiations, and that accepted taking into account the opinion of Parliament regarding the Commissioners, as well as the reshuffling of the Commissioners.
This means, once and for all, and in my view, that the Commission has so far demonstrated its willingness to maintain a close relationship with Parliament.

Demonstrating it made clear, evident, and unmistakable that the strategic alliance established by the Treaty of Lisbon for the community method referred to by colleague Sariusz Wolski is the alliance between the Parliament and the Commission.
And therefore, the Commission and the Framework Agreement deserve the full support of the PPE.
Mr. President, five years after the Orange Revolution, the Ukrainians appear to view this period as a missed opportunity.
and the blame is not solely on your political elites.
It is certain that these have consumed themselves too much in power and influence struggles.
that many reforms remained unfulfilled, many never went beyond paper.
It is inconceivable that, in any democracy, electoral laws be changed between the two rounds of an election.
