does not confer legitimacy upon those who proposed them, those who voted for them, or those who promulgated them.
and translates unworthy and commendable intentions into items on its agenda.
And much needs to change due to the lack of independence of the Judiciary, which is subject to excessive influence not only from political power but also from economic actors.
Without a free and independent justice system, there is no rule of law, human rights are not guaranteed, there is no foreign investment, and there is no progress.
Yet the blame for this disillusionment also lies heavily within this Parliament and, fundamentally, with many Member States.
I would like you, for a few seconds, to imagine yourself as a citizen of a European country that, due to the twists of history, is still not a member of the European Union.
What would we expect from the Union?
solidarity.

Yet many chancelleries, solely concerned with not offending or displeasing Moscow, exploited and abused the ongoing internal conflicts in Kyiv to avoid unequivocally stating that Ukraine is an independent and sovereign country.
Thus, if that is the freely expressed will of the majority of its people and provided they meet the established criteria, Ukraine may aspire to become, in the future, a member of the European Union.
I would like to emphasize that in island regions, air transport is sometimes the only means of access, and operating conditions are constrained by the scale of their passenger and cargo flows.

While there are insular regions where the abundance of these hubs is attractive to many airlines, fostering competition and low transportation costs and thereby improving accessibility, others, such as the Azores, still have limited such hubs and are therefore less appealing to airline operators.

As a result, airfares are higher, acting as a barrier to population mobility and a deterrent in attracting tourists, thereby hindering the realization and consolidation of that vast potential for development and diversification of its economic base.
This situation is particularly detrimental during periods of crisis such as the one we are currently experiencing.

That is why temporary support is essential to stimulate increased flows, enabling the market to function properly, while also aligning these regions with high tourism potential with the objectives of the trans-European networks in connecting Europe's peripheries.

Thank you very much, Mr. President. I rise today in this Chamber with a voice choked by the distress of someone who has personally experienced and witnessed the tragedy that struck Madeira last Saturday.

My words are, of course, to associate myself with the feelings of pain and mourning that have affected the families of the deceased victims, whose number currently stands at 42, to whom I extend my tribute and respect for their losses.

The true extent of this catastrophe remains to be revealed, as search and rescue teams, who have been working nonstop since Saturday in a Herculean effort I commend, are only now beginning to reach the most isolated communities.
and there is concern that the number of victims may increase.

The scenario depicts widespread destruction, with significant material damage to access routes, roads, and entire areas reduced to rubble, as well as critical services such as water supply and electricity being severely disrupted.
Approximately 600 people have been displaced, having lost their homes and personal belongings.
The economic and social impact of this catastrophe prevents people from resuming a normal life course.
But it is now necessary to look ahead.
It is also necessary to convey a message of solidarity, hope, and trust to people.
Help is needed. It is in this chapter that the European Union, and in particular the Parliament, have a role to play. They are essential in mobilizing the Solidarity Fund.
And when we are called upon to intervene, we must do so swiftly, for we cannot ask people to wait, especially when they need us most.

I appeal here to the Commission and its President, Durão Barroso, particularly to the Commissioner for Regional Development and to Hannes Hahn, and I welcome your initiative to visit Madeira at the earliest opportunity.
asking him to also carry this message of hope, assistance, and support for reconstruction. Because now is the time to rebuild what nature, unfortunately once again, has taken from us.
But I am certain we will do it, because as the song of Madeira taught me, the people of Madeira are humble, stoic, and brave.
It is the people who, among the rocky terrain, carried the soil, heroes of labor in the Monte Agreste.
Through wood he will honor his history, and along the path of labor he will strive and achieve his well-being and glory.
We begin today's plenary session with a minute of silence.

And it was a minute of silence with two meanings: one of solidarity with the victims of this disaster that struck Madeira and their families, and another of tribute to all workers who have tirelessly assisted these victims.
But a moment of silence should make us reflect, and should also lead to a reflection that goes beyond the one we are currently engaged in here.
What we have observed in Madeira is a phenomenon that will increasingly recur.
Climate change causes extreme events to recur, and we must have responses ready for them.
We cannot prevent natural disasters, but we should not be doomed to them always having such dramatic consequences for people's lives.
That is why we must strive for planning and zoning policies that are oriented toward the public good.

But I would like to conclude, Mr. President, with what is most important and what has brought us here: the European Commission, the European Parliament, and the European institutions must have the capacity to provide an urgent response to an emergency situation. For this, the necessary financial resources and the required speed are essential.
A gesture of solidarity in memory of the families and friends of the victims of the tragic events that occurred on Saturday on the island of Madeira.
As an Asturian, as a Portuguese, and as a European, I cannot but appeal to Parliament and the Union to demonstrate active solidarity in favor of the Autonomous Region of Madeira and its populations.
It is in these regions that the presence of the Union needs to be felt most, and precisely at these moments—when its solidarity is most needed—is it also most important that it be felt.

Mr. President, I too wish to express my solidarity with the people of Madeira and to urgently appeal to the European institutions, and in particular the Commission, to unlock all available resources, all means at their disposal, in order to provide this assistance.
I would also like to emphasize that when disasters of this nature occur, it is much more difficult to address their consequences when they happen in poor countries and impoverished regions.

However, in the case of Madeira, we must also take into account that the island's economic foundation is directly affected by its reliance on tourism, resulting in a double impact when its natural beauty, accessibility, and quality of life are completely destroyed.

Therefore, there is indeed a different situation when we face problems of this severity in poor countries and in poor regions, and particularly in mountainous and tourist areas, as is the case of Madeira.
Hence, it is absolutely essential that all the adaptations that were planned, requested, and recalled here with regard to the Solidarity Fund be implemented without further delay.

that they come into immediate effect, as climate change unfortunately means these processes will recur, particularly in Europe's poorest regions, which experience torrential rainfall and extreme drought during the summer. Thank you once again, Mr. President.
Commissioner and Council, we are here discussing the reinvention of the energy system at the European scale through investment in low-carbon technologies.
I believe it is one of the very important challenges, as indeed referred to in the communication being made.
And it is one of the challenges we must address centrally. But there is no clarification here, which is why it is important to raise these questions and request clarification.
It appears that, besides the lack of clarification regarding funding, there is also a lack of political clarification.
Discussions focus on energy efficiency, carbon capture, nuclear power, biofuels, solar energy, and even consider energy efficiency as a more cost-effective way to reduce emissions.
It surprises me that there are other much cheaper ways to reduce carbon emissions that are not even mentioned in this communication, such as, for example, reducing consumption right away.
energetic, microgeneration, distribution, and different forms of distribution and different projects that do not have to go solely through large structures and access to energy.
I believe that would certainly help clarify the financial aspects, making the plan cheaper.

And therefore, within this political clarification and within this financial clarification, there remains the need to address what appears to me to be an absolutely crucial point: the emphasis that is constantly placed on public-private partnerships as if they were the solution to everything.
When asked where the money comes from, they tell us from the Commission that it comes from where it is.
I confess that I am not any clearer when told that the money will come from where it is, without being told exactly where it is.

And therefore, this emphasis on public-private partnerships—systematically advocated when discussing low-carbon technologies—leaves us with a clear certainty: knowing neither the source of the funding nor the nature of the partnership, we already know from the outset who will ultimately bear the cost.
It is the taxpayers who pay initially, and it is the consumers—who happen to also be taxpayers in the end.

And we know that those who pay less, from the outset, are private institutions and organizations, which are the ones making the investment, are compensated for it, but ultimately end up monopolizing the profits.
And without this clarification, it seems to me that, yet again, we are systematically passing on to future generations the bill for the transformation of the European energy model.

Thank you, Madam President. The European Union recently concluded, within the framework of the World Trade Organization, an agreement with countries from Latin America, in which it committed to substantially and progressively reducing tariffs on imports of bananas from these countries.

I draw your attention to the fact that such an agreement has directly negative effects on European banana producers, particularly on banana producers from Madeira in Portugal, but even more severely on the banana producers of the Canary Islands in Spain.

I recall that the situation of producers in these regions is already seriously exacerbated by the physical and orographic characteristics of the islands and by the size of their holdings, which leads them to face persistent difficulties that hinder their development.

It is therefore urgent to take measures to protect the producers of bananas, timber, and the Canary Islands, given the serious and immediate impact that the new Geneva Agreement will have on the European Union's import market, and consequently on the production and marketing of bananas from these overseas regions.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, Commissioner, as rapporteur for opinion of the Committee on Employment and Social Affairs, I would like to begin by...
Congratulations to Member of Parliament Krielov, even in his forced absence, and highlight the way in which he successfully prepared this important report, thereby facilitating the main objective.
I would also like to congratulate Deputy Creel for the presentation she made to us.

This primary objective is, without further delay, to simplify procedures and accelerate the implementation of programs funded by the Cohesion Fund, the Structural Funds, and the Regional Development Fund.
In the context of the current financial, economic, and social crisis, pressure on national financial resources has been increasing.
This pressure will decrease with better utilization of community funding, enabling faster delivery of these resources to the beneficiaries most affected by the economic breakdown.
More than 20 million Europeans are unemployed, that is, 4 million more than a year ago, and unfortunately, the trend is set to rise.

This situation requires ensuring the proper implementation of cohesion programs, as they represent the most powerful and significant lever of support for the real economy, particularly for small and medium-sized enterprises and employment.
SMEs are the engine of the European economy and the driving force behind sustainable growth and the creation of jobs in both quantity and quality.

The continuation of simplification and clarification of the rules governing cohesion policy will undoubtedly have a positive impact on the pace of program implementation, particularly because it will already

national, regional, and local authorities the possibility of resorting to clearer and less bureaucratic rules that will allow greater flexibility in adapting programs to new challenges. Thank you very much.
Thank you, Mr. President. Of course, this crisis was an unforeseen event, and of course, the precautionary principle had to take precedence.
but there are lessons to be learned, and the first is that, for five days, there was not a sufficiently strong and politically responsible European voice heard.
And that voice was missing in terms of defending passengers' interests, clarifying their rights, seeking alternative transportation options, and coordinating solutions.

A second conclusion is that the exit from the crisis cannot appear, to the eyes of the average citizen, to be a struggle between those seeking to avoid losses and those aiming to uphold the precautionary principle.
There must be clear transparency and clear objectivity regarding the conditions under which one exits a situation of complete closure of the European space.
And therefore, the improvement of scientific tests and coordination also at the level...

Thank you, Mr. President. I would also like to begin by congratulating my colleague Tikal on her work and perseverance in this highly important file, and on the efforts she has made to improve the energy performance of buildings and to create a more sustainable Europe.
Energy expenditure in the European Union, as we know, is far above what is acceptable, which is why advancing proposals of this nature is so important.
It is long past time that we adopt an ambitious policy at the European level and complement this ambitious policy with additional measures, particularly in the current crisis situation we are facing.
The construction sector is considered one of the sectors, one of the markets, with the greatest potential in terms of energy economy, but it is not only that.
And it is not just that; for this reason, it is an outstanding sector for the implementation of public policies. In this sense, Mr. President, I would like to conclude.
We therefore have the opportunity to invest in improving the energy efficiency of buildings and their potential to create millions of jobs in the coming years.
It is not only about new buildings, but also about the rehabilitation and recovery of existing buildings that are degraded or inadequate.

Let us therefore make use of this legislation as a fundamental contribution to revitalizing the European economy. We cannot continue to fail to capitalize on investments that can and must be strategic for recovery from the crisis. Let us hope that the Commission will provide investment, and that Member States will know how to make the most of it. Thank you very much.

Thank you very much, Mr. President, Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues. I would like to begin by congratulating my colleague Manders on the work he has carried out in this report, as well as thanking the colleagues who have collaborated with him on this dossier.

In fact, the mere fact that this proposal simplifies and improves the existing regulatory framework—by consolidating into a single regulation all current legislation, including three foundational directives on the designation and labeling of textile products—is in itself a significant development.

Moreover, beyond this, the proposal will have a positive impact on private stakeholders and public authorities, and will also provide a framework for new fibers developed, thus encouraging innovation in the textile and apparel sector.
This review will also enhance the transparency of the process for adding new fibers to the list of harmonized fiber names.
There can be no doubt that the parties involved will gain advantages from this regulation.
Let us see, the textile industry will benefit from cost savings in administration and the possibility of marketing new fibers earlier.
On their part, the authorities of the Member States will not need to transpose the changes into national legislation, thereby enabling a significant cost saving for these States.
And consumers will have the assurance that fibers so labeled meet specific characteristics, while also benefiting from the fact that the new fibers reach the market earlier.
For the European industry, the introduction of these new fibers is important as a means of promoting innovation, while also increasing the level of information provided to consumers.
In the current context of a globalized world, emerging economies, with increasingly expanding markets in which millions of products produced by millions of companies circulate,
The bet on innovation and the speed with which this innovation reaches consumers is absolutely crucial for companies, especially for small and medium-sized enterprises.
that the application of this regulation may enhance its competitiveness.
These small and medium-sized enterprises will undoubtedly gain market share with this new regulation, and through this means will contribute to increasing employment in Europe.
Thank you very much, Madam President, Madam Minister, Commissioner. The shortage of organs for transplantation is a growing problem.

As already mentioned here on several occasions, this is a major issue that raises concerns not only of public health but also related matters such as the strengthening of the black market and organ trafficking, neither of which enhances our healthcare systems nor our citizens.

And therefore, we must combat this crime, and combat it through legislation such as the proposals we are discussing here. In my view, this is not only more than important and more than relevant, but essential; and for this reason, I wish to focus on the most significant dimensions and issues raised by these two proposals.
because the system we have so far, and without these two proposals, is a system of enormous inequalities.
inequalities among member states, inequalities among existing systems, inequalities in access to organs, inequalities among citizens—these, it seems to me, we simply cannot sustain.
Therefore, it is important to emphasize that these inequalities are decreasing. And these proposals aim precisely to combat this.
I believe that a European system that nonetheless maintains certain specificities of some countries, so that they are not forced to adopt more restrictive systems, is the solution to this problem.
And the work carried out by the rapporteurs, Mr. Mikolasik and Mr. Perello Rodrigues, constitutes work of extreme importance and valuable contribution to the issue we are facing.
If we are discussing proposals based on and supported by issues such as gift-giving, volunteering, gratuitousness, confidentiality, and data protection,

Traceability, accountability, and here I believe it is important—on the Parliament's side—that this extends all the way to patient recovery, and not merely to donation; and to cooperation among Member States. I believe we are on the right path.
I should only mention, however, with regard to the notions among the living, I am pleased by the inclusion and the expansion, this expansion achieved with rights.

obviously ensuring all rights and all conditions, but very importantly, having gone beyond the limited cycle of the conventional family, because otherwise it would merely be another form of achieving other forms of equality.

Therefore, promoting data as the central focus of transplants and safeguarding patient data and security seems to me more than sufficient reason for all of us to stand together tomorrow and vote in favor of these two proposals of utmost importance for the European Union.

Mr. President, Minister, and Commissioner, the ability to replace deteriorated human organs with organs from another person, while preventing rejection, is one of the great achievements of modern medicine.

European benchmarking reveals differences, showing that medium-sized countries like Portugal perform very well in such comparisons, while larger countries like Spain are now recognized as leaders in organization and best practices.
some principles and practices to preserve.

First, strengthening scientific knowledge and information that increasingly encourage living donation and promote widespread post-mortem donation. Second, the prohibition of the commercialization of...

gift, not only among member states but also beyond them, preventing transplant tourism and combating organ trafficking. Thirdly, the proactive promotion of donation in hospitals as a means of overcoming scarcity.

Finally, Madam President, ensuring equal access to transplantation for all who need it and meet clinically harmonized criteria, in accordance with the principle of universal access to health, the cornerstone of the social Europe we are proud of.
I congratulate the rapporteurs on the work accomplished.
I would like to emphasize that it is with great, shall we say, satisfaction that the European People's Party and the Portuguese deputies of the European People's Party
European, this process of accession to the European Union's adherence to the European Convention on Human Rights is now beginning.

We, a country such as Portugal, which has consistently been at the forefront of human rights—abolishing slavery as early as 1761, and pioneering the abolition of the death penalty—can evidently only be deeply interested in and supportive of this accession process.

I would, however, like to draw attention to the fact that we consider it very important for the European Parliament to follow the European Union's accession negotiations to the European Convention on Human Rights step by step.

We consider this aspect, from a technical, legal, and political standpoint, to be fundamental, since Parliament is also a House of Human Rights and a House where human rights hold great significance. I would now like to emphasize what we consider to be very important.

For us members of the European Parliament, who maintain contact with many countries outside the European Union, it is very important—it sends a very positive signal—to the members of the Council of Europe, particularly those who are not members of the European Union, regarding the Union's accession.
For these members of the Council of Europe, it is a sign of strengthened commitment by the European Union to its situation and to levels of democracy and respect for fundamental rights.
Madam President, Mr. Commissioner, social housing is an economic service of general interest with an important role in the market, generating positive externalities that benefit society as a whole.
According to Protocol 26 attached to the Treaty of Lisbon, it is the responsibility of national, regional, and local authorities to carry out and organize this type of service.
Hence, it is little comprehensible that the target audience is restricted to families with incomes below 33,000 euros.

It is a measurement of poverty that is made in absolute and purely financial terms, failing to account for the diversity among the 27 member states, reducing the issue to a simplistic assessment of solvency.

Now, social housing addresses cultural issues, social concerns, proximity to places of work, the significant redefinition of women's roles in the labor market, and worker mobility across European space.
The struggle the European Union is undertaking to overcome the current crisis hinges on economic growth, which in turn drives an increase in immigration and ethnic minorities.
economic and cultural factors, and the need for their integration, even amidst the risks of ghettoization associated with it, which we must combat with just balance.
Is the Commission not encroaching upon the competence of the Member States?

Madam President, Madam Commissioner, esteemed colleagues, allow me, Madam President, to highlight the fact that we are discussing the first report from the Committee on Agriculture, subject to the new ordinary legislative procedure provided for by the Treaty of Lisbon.

This is a historic moment that, by confirming this Chamber's voting intent as I propose, will bestow a proper beginning to this new phase of shared competences in agricultural matters within the European Union.
I am speaking precisely of co-legislating on your overseas regions, as a practical demonstration of the commitment to building a Union strengthened by cohesion.
in the spirit of active solidarity, respecting the specificities and differences among its parts, as the only viable path toward building our common European project.

I also speak by introducing essential regulation for the viability of the already century-old sugar industry in the Azores, the region from which I come, which is why I particularly congratulate this initiative, thus contributing to a...
healthy agricultural diversification in a region where the dairy sector, as we know, holds a unique, irreplaceable national significance that we must sustain and strengthen.
Madam President, I would like to thank Commissioner Ciolos and the high-level officials from the Commission with whom we have worked and negotiated for the openness and flexibility demonstrated.
The Spanish Presidency of the Council also made unrestrained efforts to turn our shared desire to reach an agreement in first reading into reality.

This gratitude is, of course, extended to the Shadow Members of Parliament from the other political groups, as well as to the excellent staff of the Secretariat of this Parliament's Committee on Agriculture, whose collaboration enabled us to reach a result that I consider highly positive.

I would also like to highlight the decisive contribution made by the Agricultural Federation of the Azores and by the regional and Republic governments in their involvement and commitment to achieving a solution for the Azores sugar issue.

The presentation of the Council's draft regulation on October 2nd, before the Committee on Agriculture, took place at a time when there were no prospects regarding the entry into force of the Lisbon Treaty, which continued to await ratification by all Member States.
In this context, the initial idea of the Commission and the Council was that the proposal be approved through a simplified procedure, without any amendments, to take effect as of 1 January 2010.

With the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon, and despite, at the time, prevailing opinion among other political groups, as well as the Council and the Commission, that the proposal should remain in the simplified procedure, as they considered that...

The proposal did not require any amendments and should enter into force promptly. Although I personally hold this view, my group and the Socialists and Democrats believe that Parliament should already express its position within the framework of the Treaty of Lisbon, fulfilling its role as co-legislator.

We did so at an opportune moment, because from then on new possibilities emerged, first through the contribution of the Committee on Regional Development and the opinion of colleague Nunta Cheira, whom I would like to take this opportunity to...

to congratulate and thank for the cooperation always provided throughout this entire process, and then with my report introducing the new opportunities for sugar in the Azores, as well as with the contribution and support of my colleagues from the Committee on Agriculture.

I would like to say, finally, in this first intervention, that regarding the amendments proposed by the Committee on Regional Development and voted on by the Committee on Agriculture and which I supported, we were unable to secure their acceptance in the compromise reached during the trilogue.

It was essential to secure agreement at first reading, thereby safeguarding the improvements already achieved and the need for entry into force without committing to retroactivity as of January of this year.
However, since the Commission is already preparing a review document for the ESF+, we will certainly have the opportunity to discuss it again very soon.

Thank you, Mr. President, Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues. We are facing an acceleration of a global transformation that has devastating consequences for our economic, political, and social systems, and consequently for all our citizens.
Today we witness an unprecedented weakening of states' capacity to respond.
The European Union consequently needs to identify common causes and allies and act clearly and unitedly on the world stage.
Extraordinary situations require clarity in joint action.

If we do not take the rigorous and collective accountability measures that are required, Europe will be condemned to marginalization and impoverishment. Only a strong Europe, one that respects its common rules, will be able to adequately respond to the new times.
Weak and heavily indebted states lack the capacity to protect their citizens.

Consequently, we need to be able to regain citizens' trust, win the battles concerning stability, budgetary discipline, job creation, the stability of the Monetary Union, globalization, and strategic choices.
This, under penalty of compromising our future.
and one secures the future by betting on social cohesion, ensuring peace, and building a new model based on the values of freedom, social justice, and responsibility.
Thus, the 2020 Strategy and the integrated Europe 2020 guidelines constitute essential instruments for a new cycle of growth and employment in Europe.
For proper implementation and execution, it is necessary to establish clear and measurable objectives in the areas of employment, education, and poverty reduction.

It is also decisive for the success of the strategy that everything possible be done to facilitate the transposition by Member States of their national objectives and to ensure the proper implementation of this strategy. Thank you very much.
When the Economic and Monetary Union celebrated its 10th anniversary, the Commission regarded the euro as the Union's most successful project.
Today we would have difficulty asserting this without reservations, as the crisis has clearly demonstrated that the Economic and Monetary Union is an unfinished project.
Minimal rescue measures were taken in an emergency context, but let us not deceive ourselves. The conditions of pressure, the direct interests of the main parties.
member countries. The painful nature of the decisions, along with their slowness, weakened the image of the euro and the European Union in the eyes of citizens and around the world.
We must face the facts.
The European Union and the euro are a process of integration, not merely cooperation among countries.
They are an unfinished process that at this moment either strengthens or faces serious risks of disappearing.
There are three themes that must be addressed. First, it is important to consolidate the instruments that have been launched in this climate of emergency. A monetary fund needs to be established.

European, it is necessary to move forward with Eurobonds, it is necessary to advance with the architecture of European supervision and regulation, and it is necessary to advance with a European dimension in the management of banking crises.
Second dimension: it is important to recognize that there is no strong currency if the economy is weak. The 1% growth projected for the European Union is unsustainable in terms...
both in terms of its demographic composition and in combating unemployment. The 2020 Strategy is nothing more than a set of good ideas without the means for implementation.

Third dimension, Mr. Commissioner Lirenne: fiscal consolidation is important, but it cannot coexist with growing divergences among the Member States comprising the Union. The convergence policy must be urgently revisited. Thank you very much, Mr. President.
Thank you very much, Mr. President. Several of my colleagues from the PPE have already provided a very comprehensive overview of the situation.
I believe that now it will be necessary to demand and to make reference to the role of national states.
In fact, national governments also have a role here in imposing requirements within their respective states.
and the conditions under which the single currency can be strengthened and this project of monetary and economic integration can be advanced.
And here I would like to say that our party, the party linked to the PPE, which is the PSD in Portugal, is supporting the government's austerity measures, because we consider

that the mistakes made by the Socialist government in Portugal over these past fifteen years, which are now unfortunately visible to all, must be corrected, and that these corrections will have the support of the PSD.

But we are supporting the austerity measures; we also criticize the Portuguese government, and it serves as a good example for other similar cases because it is not reducing expenditure. And what is essential is not merely...
increase taxes to reduce the deficit, but rather reduce expenditure—require the government to have the courage to take measures that cut spending, ease the deficit, and alleviate public debt.
This applies to Portugal and to other countries that are currently in a difficult situation. And we, as European parliamentarians, have the responsibility to also do so in our respective countries.
apology for the necessary measures to ensure the euro emerges stronger, and to enable this space of integration and this dream—this dream of the PPE and this dream of us all—to be realized.
Therefore, we criticize those governments that lack the courage to take the necessary measures within their own states.

Dear colleagues, I would like to begin by saying that, in our opinion, the report on the assessment of the results of the 2006–2010 action plan for gender equality, which aims to influence future strategy on this matter, could have been more decisive and less controversial.

In fact, the approach taken toward several ethically sensitive issues—particularly the issue of women's sexual and reproductive health—should have been different if the genuine objective had been to achieve the broadest possible consensus.

That said, I would like to emphasize that during periods of so-called normal economic growth, concern regarding women's rights—particularly the disparities between men and women in the workplace, society, and the family—is significant. However, these problems are profoundly exacerbated by an economic and social crisis such as the one we are currently experiencing.
Hence the need for a strengthened strategic commitment by the Member States, at the level of local and regional authorities and community institutions, regarding protection

to say that, in line with what President Dole and colleague Werner Langen stated, it is essential that there be an economic government of the Union, but that this economic government should be centered on the Commission.

This is the first point I wished to emphasize. And having said this, I would add that we should not merely, as has been done here this entire morning, focus our debate on immediate issues, but rather...
also in those pertaining to the 2020 Strategy. And I would particularly emphasize the issues of education and training for young people, the issues of innovation, research, and development.

because these are the niches that will enable Europe to be competitive in the global arena. And today I must say, unfortunately, that the European educational model has failed, and it has failed because, when compared to the models of emerging countries,

Like India, like China, like Brazil, it is today a less demanding and less rigorous model. And therefore I also ask that, within the boldness of this idea of innovation, development, research, and education, there be rigor and high standards within the context of European educational systems.
Only with this rigor and high standards will we prepare our youth to compete in the global market.

We cannot focus solely on this year's crisis or the next two or three years; we must think in terms of a strategy for Europe over the next 10 to 20 years, and that strategy depends on an education system based on rigor and high standards.
Mr. Commissioner, never before has the European project, built with such effort over the past 50 years, been so threatened.

The frustration of European citizens is immense. The financial system, which was meant to serve them, has destroyed their savings, their pensions, their investment plans, their jobs, in short, their future.
Regarding the necessary strengthening of the entire financial services market—banking, insurance, securities—the path to follow is clear and lies in the architecture we are discussing here today.
We actually need a strong European supervisory architecture, covering all products and actors, equipped with methodologies and tools for...
effective intervention and one capable of responding to the reality of a market that, particularly in the European context, does not recognize national boundaries.

Risk cannot and should not be eliminated from the financial market, but we do need regulation that makes this risk more transparent and prevents the accumulation of bubbles whose bursting would affect the entire system and spill over into the real economy.

But if, despite these safeguards, new crises occur in the future, it is crucial to have mechanisms in place that limit contagion, particularly with regard to the banking system, as it is vital for the functioning of the economy and the guardian of citizens' savings.
Here lies, precisely within the powers and competences of the Banking Authority, the ability to implement a new strategy for managing cross-border banking crises—a topic addressed in my report.
It is not within our power to prevent bank failures, but rather to ensure that their restructuring or eventual liquidation is carried out in a...
ordered manner, limiting side effects on the rest of the system, and especially avoiding placing the burden of crisis costs on taxpayers.

To achieve this, a set of new instruments is required. It is necessary to adopt common rules, definitions, methodologies, tools, and terminology for all parties involved in restructuring, reorganization, or liquidation operations.
It is necessary to harmonize the relevant national legal frameworks for crisis management, particularly bankruptcy and liquidation laws.
It is necessary to grant supervisors broader powers of intervention.
It is necessary to establish clear criteria, not only quantitative but also qualitative, that enable interventions to be initiated in an institution facing difficulties.
Contingency plans need to be prepared.
This entire process requires time for 27 member states.
However, approximately 50 banks out of the 12,000 operating in Europe account for 70% of the assets.
Now, this means an enormous concentration of risk, but also an opportunity to intervene prioritarily on them.

There are, therefore, conditions justifying the proposal of a special and urgent process for coordinating banks that, due to their cross-border nature, size, and relationships with other banks, may have systemic impact on the financial system.
Now, for these banks, it is possible to anticipate a special supervision by the new European Banking Authority, although operating within the College of National Supervisors.
It is possible and urgent that they, in view of the risks they pose, begin contributing to a fund that finances potential interventions.
that are necessary, and it is important that the Banking Authority establish a specialized unit to coordinate interventions in the event of difficulties in these banks.
Collectively, the proposed elements ensure that interventions in systemically significant transnational banks are consistent and coherent among
different countries, and above all, that a principle so cherished in Europe be upheld—that the costs of pollution caused by these banks be borne by the polluter themselves.
This proposal fits perfectly within what we are discussing, particularly regarding the competences of the Banking Authority. Indeed, I would say it is the most obvious test of its effectiveness.
In the case of the vote in the Economic and Monetary Committee, there was a very large majority in support of this proposal, 87% of the votes.
We also count here in the plenary with the overwhelming vote of this institution, in the sense that, by strongly supporting this forward-looking, realistic, yet pragmatic proposal, we...
Let us take an action that is urgent and necessary to rekindle Europeans' hope in Europe, in its financial system, and in particular in the banking system. Thank you very much.
Thank you. Briefly, to emphasize only four points.
The first is that it was an extraordinary experience working in such a collaborative manner with my colleagues from the other political forces.
Thank you very much for including me in the supervision package, and in particular, thank you to Mr. Garcia Margalho for all the collaboration that was possible to obtain.
Secondly, I would like to thank my personal collaborators as well as my colleagues who contributed to the specific report entrusted to me, which enabled me to incorporate 90% of their proposals.

Deforestation, as we know, is one of the planet's major environmental and social problems, and each year we lose 13 million hectares of forest, thereby contributing to 20% of global carbon emissions and posing a significant threat to biodiversity.

The contribution of deforestation to climate change is immense, and as such, it is highly responsible for the occurrence of extreme weather events, floods, and even the desertification of vast areas of the planet.
Forest loss endangers all of humanity, but this risk and its impact are not shared equally among us.
Communities with fewer resources and those that rely most heavily on forests and nature for survival are the most affected.
Deforestation concerns our collective existence, the scarcity of natural resources, their distribution, the rights of local and indigenous communities, and social justice.
The primary cause of deforestation is illegal logging, which accounts for 20% to 40% of global industrial wood production.
Therefore, we are also talking about the transfer of wealth from wood-producing countries to wood-consuming countries.
This illegal practice jeopardizes the sustainable exploitation of wood and the companies that engage in it.
Beautiful words are not enough.
This Parliament must take strong and decisive measures in this fight.
This report takes a very significant step in this direction by establishing a ban on the trade of timber.
Good intentions alone are not enough either.
Effective means are required, namely sanctions, which in this case may take three forms.

First, fines that effectively eliminate the economic benefit derived from illegal practices, increased in case of recurrence. Second, confiscation of the timber from the products in question. And third, immediate suspension of the authorization to carry out a commercial activity.

And thus it is because those who exploit or commercialize a natural resource should be socially responsible. And thus it is also because those who first place wood or wood products into the European Union should be required to exercise due diligence to ensure that the wood has been harvested legally.
That is why all subsequent operators must provide basic information about this wood, namely its origin and its destination.
The public must also have the right— and I conclude here, Mr. President—the right to access information about who is complying and who is not complying. Thank you very much.

There are regions within the Union that face permanent constraints that strongly hinder their economic and social development.

The outermost regions, united by their particular characteristics, also differ among themselves in terms of their levels of development.

Thank you very much, Mr. President. I would like to begin by congratulating my colleagues Luan and Manescu on the outstanding work they have carried out in their respective reports.

Today, more than ever, achieving economic, social, and territorial cohesion is essential for affirming the competitiveness of the European Union on a global scale.
This goal will only be achieved if we recognize that only by reinforcing and developing the regional dimension can we have a cohesive Europe internally and a competitive Europe externally.

In this regard, I would highlight three points that I consider crucial. First, decentralization, ensuring that local and regional authorities, particularly those with legislative powers, participate more actively and contribute to a more effective implementation of cohesion policy.

Member States inviting regional and local authorities to participate in negotiations on the future of structural funds on equal footing with national authorities and representatives. Another aspect I consider crucial is the request for a simpler architecture for the post-2013 funds, not as a consequence of the economic crisis, but as a general principle of future cohesion policy, in order to facilitate the absorption of funds.
The underlying concern behind the Treaty of Lisbon was to bring Europe closer to its citizens.

I now yield the floor to Mr. Rangel. You have one and a half minutes. Thank you, Madam President. The first point I would like to make clear here—and one that is important for us to reinforce in this Parliament—is that...
It is that, contrary to what sometimes appears to be read in the press and in statements by certain European leaders, the euro, as a currency, has been fundamental in addressing the current crisis.

Without the euro, we would be in an extremely difficult situation, with the currencies of the weakest countries experiencing massive devaluation and an eventual German mark facing an appreciation impossible to sustain for both the German economy and the European economy.
And thus, the euro has been a factor of stability, particularly not only for countries within the eurozone but also for the currencies of countries that chose not to join the eurozone.

And precisely for this reason, because we must defend this community that has responded to the crisis better than any other, and as we will see in the future—for example, what will happen to the dollar and the United States—we will observe how the euro truly has advantages.

We now have the responsibility in this Council to do everything possible to defend the euro, in particular to establish a stabilization fund based on the community method and capable of introducing responsibility in...

Thank you, Mr. President. The price of food has reached its all-time high and is increasing for the seventh consecutive time.
There are several factors that may explain this increase, the first of which is a short-term factor.
It has to do with the coincidence of poor weather conditions in certain countries, such as Russia and Argentina,
but there are two factors that are structuring, of a structural nature, and that is precisely why I bring these two factors here.
The first concerns the fact that some food producers and consumers who were exporters have become importers, as is the case with China.
And the second relates to the fact that the weight of some major multinational corporations enables them to manipulate prices in their favor.
And the example of cereals is very clear at this level. It is dominated by six major multinationals.
This situation therefore places farmers in considerable uncertainty regarding food product prices.
Europe must therefore resume doing what it has neglected for some time, namely, protecting its producers and consumers.
In a world where everyone resorts to protective measures and multinational corporations manipulate prices, we cannot afford to remain naive. Thank you very much.

We aim not only to ensure a high level of protection for European consumers, but also that European businesses, regardless of their size, can supply goods and services to consumers in the 27 Member States without unnecessary legal barriers.

This new directive will put an end to the legislative fragmentation that has been undermining the participation of our companies in the cross-border market and will provide a significant boost to the internal market.
I highlight the new provisions regarding information to be provided to the consumer, requiring that such information be clear, comprehensible, and supplied in a timely manner.

right from the outset by affirming the absolute necessity of defending our founding values in relations with neighboring countries.

Tailored to each state, with clear benchmarks and careful assessment, paving the way toward a future Mediterranean economic space with the new democracies of the South.

To conclude, Mr. Commissioner, we trust that the positive atmosphere and cooperation that have characterized work on this issue will lead to the European Parliament's ongoing involvement in the planning and evaluation of this policy. Thank you very much.

Mário, rapporteur, first of all, I would like to congratulate here, in particular, the work of our PPE colleague, Mário David, whom I am representing today, as I am absolutely...

We even consider that work should continue in order to help stabilize the situation of so many millions of affected people.
What we cannot accept is that the European Union's trade policy is now regarded as an appropriate and suitable instrument for humanitarian aid.

The truth is that this brings two detrimental consequences. One consequence within the European Union, particularly for Southern countries and their textile and clothing industries, today highly weakened by an economic, social, and financial crisis unparalleled since the...

entry into the Union. And, secondly, at the global level, in WTO negotiations, where this opening by the world's largest trading bloc, the European Union, to potential Pakistani exports, could completely disrupt the negotiations.

Mr. President, Commissioner, I would like to begin by saying that, naturally, I consider the agreement reached for Portugal to be a balanced one.
It is a very harsh agreement for the populations, but it fundamentally emphasizes structural reforms aimed at increasing growth and competitiveness.
And I believe that this is very positive.

I do not adopt here the stance of others who believe that countries such as Greece, Ireland, and Portugal did everything correctly, made no mistakes, did not overreach, did everything well, and that only the international crisis caused their problems.

No, our governments have made mistakes and pursued wrong policies. But this does not mean that there is not now an opportunity within the context of the Union to once again have the possibility of returning to the right path and the correct course.

And therefore, it is important to emphasize here that, in fact, one must tell public opinion, which opposes supporting countries in difficulty, that they are not giving money to these countries, but merely lending money to them.
but they are lending them at very high interest rates, and therefore are conducting a transaction that is beneficial for their budgets and their populations.

But I also say here that perhaps it is time to have a comprehensive European response, through the introduction of eurobonds, and the possibility of issuing them in the primary market to address international markets.

It might also be the right moment for the European Commission, the Fund, and the IMF to review their interest rates, so as to enable sustained growth in these countries currently facing difficulties by lowering those rates.

Thank you. I now give the floor to Mr. Correia de Campos for one minute. Thank you very much, Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, and Madam Rapporteur. This strategy is important for all European countries, especially those whose economies lack greater competitiveness.

Madam President, Commissioner, I am directly addressing the proposal you have presented here today to Parliament, and I must say with complete frankness that I believe some of us have every right to feel disappointed by this proposal, because the proposal put forward is not only weak, but also riddled with serious gaps.
I believe this is a European issue, not one concerning states with nuclear industries versus those without; it is a matter for all of Europe, and Europe desperately needs good news.

Now, when these tests are carried out as the Commissioner has presented them—that is, a first phase conducted by the interested parties themselves, a second phase carried out by the Member States that...

We know this is not the case, and therefore I sincerely hope there will be serious consequences, so that we can tell our citizens that we are genuinely safeguarding their security, and not merely pretending to do so. Thank you very much.

and greater responsibility than that which existed at the previous Council, where an issue was merely postponed for three months due to electoral calendars.

Thank you very much, Madam President, Commissioner. My first words are to commend the work carried out by the rapporteur, my colleague Giorgio Stavrakakis, who has done outstanding work in identifying future synergies for enhanced effectiveness among the various structural funds.

Your report also contributes to this joint debate on cohesion policy, a discussion that assumes growing significance not only because of the subject's substance, but also and above all due to the timing of its consideration.
Indeed, we are currently in the process of examining the Commission's proposal, not only regarding the new financial perspectives but also concerning the future cohesion policy beyond 2013.
Cohesion policy is a fundamental policy for the European Union.

It is a horizontal policy that, through its primary instruments—structural funds—finances projects and programs in the Member States, aiming to reduce the disparities between the development levels of various regions.
Experience, however, has shown us the efficiency and results challenges arising from the fragmentation and complexity of this policy.

Hence, a single strategic framework for the various funds, based on an integrated, results-oriented approach, is the starting point for greater and better synergies among the various instruments and for strengthening their effectiveness.
It is also essential for better coordination among various sectoral policies and for improved efficiency in the allocation of resources.
We must seize the danger of crisis to seek new alternatives.

And it is certain that all these reports currently under discussion here will pose significant challenges. We also know that to meet these challenges, we need a sound budget. But we must make a clear distinction between budgetary aspects and political objectives.
The political objectives are well known, and indeed, only by asserting these political objectives can we offer a future to the regions.

And if that is indeed the case, then we must place the budget at the service of the future of regions and citizens, rather than placing citizens and regions at the service of the budget. Thank you very much.
Mr. President, cohesion policy is the key policy for the future of the European project.
The construction of a common space, with an internal market and a shared currency, requires a certain level of convergence among its member states and regions in order to function effectively.
Current competitiveness disparities and development asymmetries have inevitably led to the weakening of the European space and to the crisis affecting the eurozone.
That is why this vital need for convergence absolutely requires a strong cohesion budget that cannot be weakened to finance other policies.
The European Union, to do more, must have more. And this is achieved by growing its budget, not by reducing it—even skillfully—especially the cohesion budget.

I therefore ask the Commissioner whether the establishment of the Infrastructure Fund and the new Innovation Policies, which will be linked to cohesion and regional development—something I support—cannot, without an increase in the community budget, turn into a Trojan horse introduced into the cohesion budget.

Modernization and renewal of energy infrastructure are essential factors for achieving the sustainable and smart growth objectives we pursue under the 2020 Strategy, as well as for our energy security, which we require.
It is essential to involve all regions in this challenge.

To achieve this, the incentives provided by regulatory systems must be improved, particularly those applicable to higher-risk projects and more innovative initiatives, such as those being developed in the Azores Islands in the field of renewable energy.

Regions should be associated with the conception and implementation phases of projects, in defining priorities, as well as in their preparation and execution, as this ensures better outcomes and, consequently, a stronger defense of the European interest.
The current economic situation further underscores the need to adopt an innovative and integrated approach to energy issues, taking into account all economic, environmental, and social aspects.
It is essential not to lose sight of its medium- and long-term effects, while striving to ensure all European citizens have access to safe, sustainable, and affordable energy.

The report we are discussing is of particular importance, given the influence that general interest social services have on the daily lives of European citizens, as well as on social cohesion, job creation, and economic growth.

Collaboration with rapporteur Proncias de Roça has been highly beneficial and clarifying, as the rapporteur himself just stated, enabling the adoption of several compromises in the Committee on Employment and Social Affairs.
Given the outcome we have here today and the discussion we have carried out, I congratulate and commend the rapporteur.
In fact, on our part, the main concern was to make clear that the competences in this matter belong to the Member States.
National authorities are entrusted with the responsibilities of defining, organizing, and funding social services of general interest.
Thus, there is no need for the creation of harmonizing legislation at the European level, given the great diversity of these general interest social services across the 27 Member States.

Simply try accessing healthcare, social security, employment, or social housing services—these are examples demonstrating that proximity and understanding of local contexts are fundamental to the proper and fair delivery of such services. Thank you very much.

Mr. President, as rapporteur for the EESC opinion on the future of social services of general interest, I would like to congratulate my colleague from Roça, highlighting the fact that we are facing an essential pillar of the social market economy model we advocate for Europe, in which the SIG plays a fundamental role in ensuring its cohesion.

Social cohesion at the level of the European Union advises against overlooking the existence of minimum protection levels in all Member States, without prejudice to respect for the competences of their national, regional, and local authorities.

European regions play a very important role in delivering these services to populations, and their views should be taken into account, given the inherent challenges they face, in order to ensure the provision of these services to the populations.

At a time of crisis such as the one we are currently experiencing, social services of general interest constitute a social protection network for the most disadvantaged or for those whose incomes do not allow them access to private services.

Yes, it is important to ensure that member states, when decentralizing powers, also provide the corresponding financial resources, so that regional and local authorities can effectively deliver services to the population.
Ms. Ashton, I begin by saying that it is obvious we must continue to support the democratic revolutions taking place in these countries.

The situation in Syria is serious; this applies to Syria, but it also applies to Yemen and Bahrain.

If we wish to avoid civil war, the last thing these countries need is for Europe to interfere in their internal affairs, and above all, very particularly, through the proposal of military or paramilitary solutions.
And if I say this, I say it because we must completely reject what is currently happening in Libya.
NATO and the United Nations have surpassed all resolutions, even those adopted by the United Nations Security Council.
And it is this silence regarding Libya, this condemnation that we must express, and it is precisely because it is absent from this resolution that I cannot support it. Thank you very much.

neighborhood policy, not only to the east, but also along the southern border, where I believe a challenge is currently unfolding—one that may be more demanding than the one we face to the east, which is longer-term and less immediate.

Thank you very much, Mr. President. I begin by congratulating the rapporteur and all those who contributed to achieving this work.
There is no doubt that this report constitutes and will constitute a reference point regarding recommendations and solid, coordinated responses to the challenges posed by the crisis.
If the conclusion that can be drawn is that the crisis we are experiencing will only be overcome through the deepening of European integration.

In a period of severe financial, economic, and social difficulties, and of painful austerity measures in some Member States, such as in my country, Portugal, there is a temptation to retreat into a protectionist and nationalist stance.
But I know one mistake.

The European response to the crisis requires a genuine European economic government committed to sustainable growth, promoting job creation and developing the social market economy, providing long-term solutions with clearly defined objectives that member states can identify with.
The strategy for exiting the crisis needs to be funded with a sufficiently ambitious community budget.
To prevent a new sovereign debt and euro crisis, closer coordination in budgetary policies is required, as well as the establishment of a system for issuing public debt securities and eurobonds.
I conclude by emphasizing that during this difficult period, it is also necessary to maintain levels of social protection, particularly for the most vulnerable citizens. Thank you very much.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, Presidency in Office, it is commonplace to say that crises help strengthen Europe.
Historically this was the case, but today I am no longer so certain that this continues to be true.

The slow, improvised, reactive manner in which the Union is responding to the Greek crisis worsens the situation every day and spreads contagion and distrust in the Union and its currency in a frightening manner.
This despite the problems of the eurozone stemming from obvious flaws in the construction of the single currency, which today concentrate on its most vulnerable links.

For the current crisis to strengthen the Union, a strategic vision is required—one that was present in the past, but which unfortunately neither the Commission nor the Council have managed to impart to the European programme and project.
This report from the Parliament, however, gives us this perspective: more Europe, and not less, is the solution.
The Parliament recalls that the market and the single currency generate unbearable divergences among economies and that these must be countered by specific policies.

It acknowledges that the functioning of the euro requires a strengthened European budget backed by own resources, and calls for a European debt issuance agency capable of protecting member states from market speculation.
and provide them with credit at reasonable prices and terms. It also acknowledges that diplomas and the mobility of workers across countries require mutual recognition of social rights and pensions.
The Parliament has united to send clear messages. Let us hope the Commission and the Council hear them.

Madam President, Madam Commissioner, dear fellow rapporteur, first of all, I would naturally like to congratulate and commend the rapporteur, as I believe this report is extremely useful and indeed enables us to take new steps forward in the field of mediation.
The report provides an overview, a range of solutions adopted by the various member states in implementing, applying...
Directive 2852-CE of the European Parliament and of the Council of 21 May 2008 concerning currently mediation in civil and commercial matters.
Indeed, we are all aware of the advantages of mediation.
On one hand, it is an extrajudicial method of conflict resolution, contributes to relieving court backlogs, rationalizes the justice system, and simultaneously provides
simultaneously providing the parties with the opportunity to achieve a friendly, faster, and less expensive resolution, thereby reducing the burden of the litigation in which they are engaged.

That is why it is essential to reiterate the objective of Article 1 of the directive when calling for the adoption of incentive measures promoting the use of alternative dispute resolution methods, and in particular mediation.
Promoting access to and use of extrajudicial conflict resolution mechanisms contributes to improving, simplifying, and making our justice system more accessible.

It is therefore a very important challenge for the European Union and for the establishment of the area of freedom, security, and justice, I would say, for the concept of the rule of law within the European Union.
And that is why, in this context of crisis as well, I consider it very important—just as stated in the report—that public opinion and stakeholders be made aware.
judicial bodies on this issue and appeal here to the Commission and to the Commissioner to swiftly present a legislative proposal on alternative dispute resolution.
