The first point I would like to make is that, in the crisis we are currently facing—the economic and financial crisis—issues concerning smart regulation, intelligent regulation, and better legislation are central.

There is no doubt that, in the so-called structural reforms that can drive the economic and financial recovery of the European space, improvement at the level of legislation and regulation is absolutely essential.

It is evident that a Europe aspiring, on the one hand, to be dynamic and competitive must have simple and accessible regulation for businesses, and likewise, obviously, for citizens. It must therefore be oriented toward their needs.
The truth is that, in this regard, it is essential today that, first and foremost, the program for reducing administrative burdens be achieved, and that all possible efforts be made toward...

promote consultations with market agents, representative associations of citizens and businesses, and conduct impact assessments of the legislation itself—this is also extremely important, so that the...

the decision-making process could essentially be based, let us say, on the views of those who are the intended recipients of the legislation, and could be carried out without administrative burdens and with lower economic and financial costs.

It is important to state that, for the Committee on Constitutional Affairs in particular, the concept of proportionality and subsidiarity is relevant—that idea that European intervention should be appropriate and not one that promotes the complexity of the system.

legal framework and the regulatory system. And therefore, in our view, we have given particular attention to the enhanced role that National Parliaments now play in this dynamic, precisely due to the principles of proportionality and subsidiarity.

That is why it is essential that, at both the level of Smart Regulation and the improvement of legislation, we now involve National Parliaments, so that both legislation and regulation are effectively addressed at the European level as well as at the national level.

be more agile, more intelligent, more efficient. And therefore, this is an excellent moment to put into practice what we announce every day: a more competitive Europe and a more agile Europe through smarter regulation.
Mr. President, the number of people without shelter, as well as those living below the poverty line, continues to rise in several Member States in recent years.
The economic, financial, and social crisis, along with rising unemployment, have exacerbated this problem, as everyone has already diagnosed.
We also all agree that this phenomenon constitutes a denial of fundamental values of the European Union, respect for human dignity and respect for fundamental rights.

In my country, for example, which is subject to a stringent austerity policy, the concept of homelessness, according to the National Plan, encompasses people who go to seek assistance in order to have a meal.
and the number of people seeking help for eating disorders has increased by more than 20% since 2008.
An increase is observed among families and individuals who have experienced a change in their living conditions due to unemployment and also due to bank debt.
This is merely an example illustrating the seriousness of this situation.
Last year, the European Parliament adopted a written declaration on an EU strategy for the homeless.

With this statement, this Parliament has highlighted the need for the European Union to adopt a sustained strategy, supported by national and regional strategies, with the long-term objective of ending the situations of people sleeping rough and the prolonged state of homelessness.
National strategies should focus on preventive measures, as well as concrete actions to improve the quality of services provided to the homeless and the availability of affordable housing.
It is also clear that this European strategy must be compatible with the social housing policies of the Member States.
We have duly noted the Commissioner's remarks and have recorded the situation as he outlined it.
I conclude by urging the European Commission to provide strong impetus to coordinate the actions of the Member States and regional authorities in combating this social injustice. Thank you very much.
Mr. President, we have been discussing the peace process in the Middle East, but is there some ongoing process that has escaped my attention?
Unfortunately, no.

The European Union repeatedly expresses, as does the majority of the international community, support for the existence of two peoples and two states sharing the space between the Jordan River and the Mediterranean, each having the right to aspire to live in peace and security.
But without dialogue between the parties, entrenching themselves in a growing accumulation of hatreds and misunderstandings that has persisted for 63 years, only extremism and frustration are fueled.

It is essential that Europe and the Arab League become much more actively involved in seeking a negotiated solution for the borders, based on those existing in 1967, with land swaps that would allow the majority of Jewish settlements to remain within Israel.

That the issue of Jerusalem, the holy city, future capital of the two States, be clarified and mutually accepted as a symbol of peaceful coexistence between two peoples who are obliged to fulfill the prophecy of Isaiah that this is the land of milk and honey.

that all countries of the Union participate in resolving the question of Palestinian refugees, and that the international community, and in particular the Arab nation, participate in the necessary financial compensation.

It remains absolutely essential that a military force under the auspices of the United Nations, and including participation by, among others, American and Islamic forces, be deployed on the ground as guarantor of peace maintenance.
Thus, let there be courage and political will.
Thank you, Mr. President. Commissioner, tourism requires a clear and coordinated European approach, with careful attention to the potentials and challenges that the various tourism modes offer us.
The tourism diversity offered by the European Union is so rich that it enables exponential growth in this sector and enhances the Union's competitiveness itself.

A bet on this key sector, which accounts for 10% of GDP and approximately 12% of employment in the European Union, will not only create added European value but also help address the economic crisis we are facing.
It comes from an overseas region, Madeira, where tourism is the main economic activity.

Therefore, the creation of a specific heading for the sector in the 2014-2020 financial framework is essential for fostering cohesive and sustainable tourism growth, which is primarily composed of small and medium-sized enterprises.

The realization of a genuine European policy on sustainable and diversified tourism requires a concrete strategic plan, with a defined timetable, actions, and funding, capable of enhancing the visibility and quality of the tourism sector in the European Union.
Thank you very much, Mr. President. The absence of an ongoing peace process is undermining basic rights and has led to unilateral measures taken by both sides that have not.
has contributed in no way to easing tensions in the region, and much less to the genuine pursuit of a life of peace, security, and dignity for the two peoples who have the right to live harmoniously.

The time has come for the international community to say enough. The time has come for Europe and the Arab League to drive the search for a lasting solution, with reason, by presenting viable options without delay and with defined deadlines.
It is evident that the United States and Russia are no longer part of the solution.
Let us begin with Jerusalem. Why not revive the original idea of a separate corpus separatum administered by the United Nations and which, of course, functions as the capital of both States?
Borders. Let us begin with those of 1967.
Swap land parcels whenever possible and appropriate.

Guard your defense of Israel, an obviously unavoidable issue for the Jewish people, for instance, by deploying blue-helmeted forces alongside Americans, Europeans, and Islamic forces along the Jordan River.
Refugees, there are approximately 4.8 million of them, and they have the right to their dignity and to a future connected to a land, preferably their own.

but they are not exclusively Israeli or Palestinian problems; they are also ours, those of the neighboring Arab countries, of the Arab community in general, and also of Europe and the rest of the international community.
colonies, having to end their expansion, and this is non-negotiable.
The announcement made today is shameful and reflects hypocrisy and bad faith in an announcement of readiness for immediate negotiations.

Above all, we want good-faith, earnest negotiations with a deadline for their conclusion, after which the international community, in order to maintain its credibility, will have to adopt a different stance.

Thank you, Madam President. Commissioner, the shameful situation in the refugee camps in Tunisia has already been mentioned here; we all share this concern, and I fully echo much of what my colleagues who have just spoken have said.
I will not refer to that in particular; I am referring to another situation that is even more overlooked—the citizens who were received in the homes of families in Tunisia.

And this is one of the situations that should make us even more ashamed, because consider this: Tunisia, in a situation of crisis, knew how to be solidary. Europe does not know how to be solidary, and is not undergoing a transition toward democracy; it did not know how to be solidary, did not know how to receive.
I believe it is, in fact, shameful that we can count the number of refugees received in Europe at only a few hundred.
Many of these people, contrary to what was said, cannot in fact return to their homes.
And there were children who were enrolled in public schools, which has implications in terms of infrastructure, and implications regarding the reception of these children in these countries.
And that is precisely why, Madam Commissioner, I am deeply moved by your appeal, and I say this not ironically, but sincerely. But please, what about the Commission?
And Europe!
What can you do?
How does Europe manage to live so well with this double standard?
We are so quick to repatriate and so slow to assist, Madam Commissioner.
It cannot continue like this.

Mr. President, I would naturally like to congratulate you on this additional hope you have given to all Members of the European Parliament, and in particular to the citizens of the European Union whom we represent here.

And having said that, I would like to emphasize that we now expect the Commission to take very strong, decisive action with the Council, so that this sovereign debt crisis can be resolved in the short term.
We know it's a marathon, but immediate signs are needed.

And therefore, without prejudice to our all beginning to consider a treaty amendment that would give political expression to the entire project we have brought forward here today, something must be done immediately.

This set of measures, and perhaps an agreement among states, what I have called a kind of parasocial agreement going beyond treaties, which could, from this moment onward, set all these ideas in motion.
Many congratulations on your courage and determination.
I believe we are on the right path and are leaving here today with renewed determination.

Mr. President, the crisis that the European Union is experiencing has had serious consequences in all Member States, and thousands of workers have been affected by dismissals caused by globalization and the crisis.
We cannot fail to express our solidarity with these workers who have lost their jobs, and to affirm that Europe must assist member states in maintaining employment rates and combating unemployment.
A swift reintegration of workers into the labor market is a priority objective.

An example of the usefulness of this fund is my country, Portugal, which has accessed it four times. Although we are aware that access to this fund could be improved, particularly in countries most affected by the crisis.

Thanks to the existence of the Globalization Adjustment Fund, it has been possible to assist thousands of workers through measures such as professional training, support for business creation, and incentives for recruitment.
This proposal is positive because it will enable Member States to request intervention from this Fund to support workers dismissed as a direct consequence of the economic and financial crisis.
We support extending the deadline and believe that the Member States most affected by the crisis should have greater and easier access to this Globalization and Crisis Support Fund.
Thank you, Mr. President. I greet the Polish Presidency and also Mr. Commissioner Ali Raz. I simply wish to say that I consider...
And today, following up on and building upon what was accomplished in the State of the Union address, a very significant step has been taken toward resolving the problems of the eurozone crisis.
because the Commission has here assumed its commitment to exercise its right of initiative and to work jointly with the Council to resolve this crisis.
I believe this is an extremely important step, and it is the only way we have at this moment to resolve the eurozone crisis.
There is no possibility of resolving it in the short term through an intergovernmental approach alone. That is why it is necessary for States, the governments of the States, to provide the...
the commission the instruments and the mandate to lead this resolution process, to implement, execute, and develop the roadmap presented here.
But I did not want to leave unsaid that this is not incompatible with our simultaneously developing, at the same time, our capacity to amend the treaties.

I am also convinced that, in the medium term, it will be necessary to amend the treaties, but even here we shall have to do so in the direction of strengthening, of giving greater power and a more prominent role to the Commission.

It is through reinforcing the community method—now in the short term, and later through treaty changes in the long term—that we will achieve greater integration and a solution for the seventeen countries of the eurozone and the twenty-seven countries of the European Union.

Thank you, Mr. President. Dr. Durão Barroso came here today to announce that one of the measures for banks lacking capital requirements is to submit plans to meet them, and if they fail to do so, they must then suspend dividends and bonuses.
I could not help but agree with the presentation made by the Mr. President.
This week we saw what happened with Dexia Bank, and just a few months ago, one of the bank's managers received a €600,000 bonus for his exceptionally outstanding management, as we all know.
But seriously, the President of the Commission must call the governments and present the complete package. And the complete package is what contains the essentials.
The essential point is that taxpayers' money should be matched by a credit policy aimed at growth.
The essential point is that banks should begin paying taxes on their profits, which does not occur in many countries, particularly in my country, Portugal.
In two years, or rather, in three years, it is the second time they are asking us to rescue the banks using money from taxpayers, from the unemployed.
Banks that save also the economy, helping to create jobs.
Mr. President, Ms. High Representative, there are moments when I believe we need little more than to take stock, and this appears to be one such moment.
It seems to me that this happens because we constantly move in step with events, reacting to one event after another, and thus somewhat lose sight of the broader perspective.

It is true that each case is unique and each country we are discussing here is a specific case, but, pardon me for saying so, Ms. High Representative, it seems difficult to discern the criteria the European Union has used in acting upon each of these cases.
We fear that we are often hostages to the interests of certain countries within the European Union, rather than all of them.
And that is why we so often use in this House the expression "double standards," and we continue to use it.

Sometimes it is sanctions yes, sometimes sanctions no. Sometimes interference goes as far as the bomb, as was the case in Libya. Other times it is total silence, as was the case with the invasion of Bahrain by the forces of...

Saudi Arabia. And, Madam Baroness, let me say that I have carefully examined all the files, and I have not yet seen a single statement from European institutions condemning the invasion of Bahrain by the military forces of Saudi Arabia.

They are death sentences, endless violence, doctors and nurses condemned to up to 30 years in prison by a military tribunal simply because they are allegedly opposed to the regime rather than helping those injured who needed them.
With regard to the dictatorial monarchy of Bahrain and the dictatorial regime of Yemen under Saleh, we have had far more silence than action.

I am not in favor of interference, never have been, but I fear we may find ourselves in an uncomfortable position—viewed from outside, Madam Baroness—which is, instead of aiding development and aspirations...

democratic and revolutionary movements of the peoples in Arab countries, instead of helping to bring about the First Arab Spring, we are preventing its spread, whether by excess or by deficiency, and I don't think we should bear that sin.
I hope that awarding the Nobel Prize to a Yemeni activist may serve as a signal that our side is the side of the peoples in revolution. Thank you very much.
Thank you, Madam President. Commissioner, this is a good report by our colleague Casa.
First of all, one must ask why revising VAT in the European Union is necessary.
First of all, the need to align it with the single market
The more regimes and VAT variations there are, the less fluid the market will be.
A VAT reform benefits e-commerce, focuses competition on the intrinsic value and base price of goods and services, and not on fiscal differences.
combatting fraud and ensuring the trust so essential, particularly in cross-border transactions, improves the economy of SMEs and reduces administrative burdens.
What other measures concerning Youth are already underway in the single market that could be linked to VAT?
First of all, a simple, clear, and transparent digital support mechanism, measure seven of the single market.
Measure 5, on services, the need to review the legislation on standardization, and the implementation of the directive on...
The new measure aimed at removing fiscal barriers and explicitly reviewing the VAT system and VAT equalization in online and face-to-face trade, which we all experience when ordering books.
The pressures for VAT reform stem from two fundamental grounds.
first of all, the economic crisis
Additional revenues are needed, including, in some countries, reducing payroll contributions to promote economic development, shifting this burden to VAT.

Secondly, the second major source of pressure is population aging, demographic aging, and the trend of shifting from indirect taxes to direct taxes, due to the reduction in the population base subject to taxation.
It is necessary to reconsider the balance between this direct and indirect taxation.
Finally, we must, in these reforms, reconcile subsidiarity with harmonization.
It is necessary to narrow the range of variation in the standard VAT rate so that we can all understand it better.
Greater transparency is needed in defining the reduced VAT rate and its scope, along with a clear and widely disseminated list of reduced rates.
It is necessary to examine exemption thresholds for SMEs, keeping in mind that each exemption inevitably brings with it a series of minor or major frauds.
Finally, welcome the Commission's recent proposal to establish a European VAT.
Madam President, I thank my colleague Rula for the excellent report she has prepared.

The advantages of public procurement are certainly well known, but the obstacles are equally evident. Conflicts of interest between European legislation and the objectives of Member States undermine competitiveness.
The dichotomy between price quality and the complexity of laws and procedures is evident.
It is essential to examine existing best practices, particularly in Portugal, where electronic procurement has functioned effectively and has facilitated public procurement.
An effective European public procurement represents a preferred means of controlling public deficits and fostering further growth.
To achieve this, it should have a stable and predictable legal framework, less bureaucratic procedures to attract SMEs, and the protection of social and environmental values.

That is why I express my satisfaction with the intentions the Commissioner Barnier has presented here today regarding the revision of public procurement, to be achieved this year as one of the 12 priorities of the Single Market Act. Thank you.
Congratulate colleague Rula and all those who collaborated with her.
Crucial in the field of public contracts is the issue of electronic public procurement.
which, unfortunately, the objectives set by the European Union—50% of decisions made through electronic procedures by 2010—were not met, standing at a mere average of 5%.
Only a few countries, such as my own, Portugal, have succeeded in this domain.

The advantages of electronic public procurement are too evident to require further elaboration and have already been outlined here. It allows replacing costly access procedures with the simple consultation of the electronic platform, thereby significantly reducing costs for businesses, especially small and medium-sized enterprises.

small and medium-sized enterprises. The dissemination via the internet and the ease of receiving procedural documents broaden the number of suppliers, increasing competition and enabling better purchases. It streamlines the process of awarding public contracts...

Easier and more flexible, it simplifies access to European Union public contracts, particularly for small and medium-sized enterprises, thereby facilitating their participation in the public procurement market—key drivers of the economy.

In this regard, we welcome and encourage the European Commission for strengthening the mechanisms aimed at transforming electronic public procurement into an increasingly comprehensive reality. Thank you very much.

We are living through decisive hours for Europe's fate. And although the situation has swiftly, before everyone's eyes, shifted from being seen as a problem of the Greeks or the Portuguese to a major European issue, the truth is that the inadequacy of the proposed solutions and their delay have produced no effective response to the rapid deterioration of the situation.
The pursuit of solutions tailored to individual states, at the expense of genuinely European solutions, allowed markets to continue speculating on sovereign debts and imposed deadlines and rhythms
absolutely impossible goals that some states are seeking to achieve through measures of a brutality previously unimaginable applied to individuals, businesses, and the public sector.

A common solution is absolutely imperative, as already found for the risk of European sovereign debts, which would alleviate market pressure and speculation and provide time for the necessary adjustment.
Without this, what we will have at the end are countries that will continue to lack the means to pay their creditors, destroyed economies, and immense suffering.

to my fellow shadow rapporteurs, with whom I have had a fruitful collaboration, and I also thank the rapporteurs from the four involved committees. These partners have made a valuable contribution to this report.
We received 400 amendments and developed 47 commitments, all of which were approved.
This demonstrates the importance and interest generated by this topic of new skills and jobs.
Ultimately, it is about preparing Europe's human resources, anticipating the needs of the labor market over the next decade.
The key strategy in preparing the report was to rephrase common ideas to avoid repetitions and ensure the report's coherence.
I believe this goal has been achieved.
We all know that the crisis currently affecting us has highlighted the weaknesses of the European economic and social model, as well as reversed many of the advancements achieved in the past.
Since the first effects of the crisis began to be felt in labor markets in 2008, Europe has already lost more than 5 million jobs.
Today, nearly 23 million people are unemployed.
Youth unemployment is even more alarming. The youth unemployment rate is near 21% and represents more than double the general unemployment rate.
On the other hand, in all member states, unemployment rates vary according to qualification levels.
This means that people with higher qualifications find it easier to secure employment.
We also know that in the near future, demands for skills, competencies, and qualifications will increase across all types and levels of profession.
In this context, Europe faces four challenges. First, job creation. Second, the need to develop and remain competitive.
Then, the sustainability of social security systems, and finally, the difficulty in finding individuals with the appropriate skills to fill job vacancies.
This report therefore has a dual objective. First, to emphasize the need for swift action to address skills gaps across Europe. Second, to better anticipate labor market needs in terms of skills.

To this end, several measures are highlighted. First, to ensure the availability of qualified labor, it is necessary to improve basic skills, as well as competencies in science, innovation, information and communication technologies, and foreign languages.
As I have already mentioned, competency needs must be identified as early as possible, within a timeframe of at least 10 years.
Today, we recognize the potential of a new sustainable economy, the green economy, and the need for investment in ecological qualifications.
Subsequently, participation in lifelong learning, professional requalification, and the reduction of school dropouts should be increased.
Another important issue is the mutual recognition of diplomas and professional qualifications, and the urgency of advancing this recognition.
Fourthly, we draw attention to the connection that must exist between educational institutions and the needs of the labor market.
Internships must be of high quality and supervised by mentors, so as to lead to employment and eliminate any potential abuses arising from the internship status.
Particular emphasis is also placed on the role of social partners and social dialogue.
The report also proposes measures to promote demand and job creation.
We have a future ahead of us, and we must create jobs.
And small and medium-sized enterprises play a decisive role in achieving this goal.

It is also necessary to strengthen the single market to facilitate the free movement of workers, thereby underscoring the need to coordinate social security systems so that workers can have their acquired rights guaranteed, particularly the portability of pensions.

Lastly, the issue of labour market flexibility. To improve the functioning of the labour market, the rules of flexicurity must be adapted to the socioeconomic contexts of each Member State. Thank you very much.
Thank you, Madam President. I begin by thanking all colleagues who have contributed to this lively debate.
All colleagues made very useful contributions and helped foster a highly constructive discussion on an ambitious agenda at a time when we are experiencing a severe crisis.
Indeed, this debate is taking place precisely on the eve of a European Council that will show whether there is or is not political will to overcome the difficulties we are experiencing.
policy for a deepening of our economic coordination, and whether there is indeed a harmonized and unified will to enable us to advance the ambition set out in this agenda. In other words, to have

New jobs in the next decade, jobs that will give hope to young people who are currently unemployed and those who are currently being trained, hope in an inclusive labor market that responds to their...

the ambition of self-worth through work, and also have the opportunity to be in a state of constant dynamism to keep pace with the challenges of a globalized economy, one with a pace unmatched by any previous generations.

Therefore, our ambition is truly to achieve job creation and economic growth, not only to address the needs of those who are unemployed as a result of the crisis, but also of those who will enter the labor market.

responding to the major challenges ahead with a green economy, services in the health sector, and services in transportation and accommodation, all of which require from each of us a different, intelligent, and inclusive vision of the labor market.
Thank you to everyone.

Marisa Matias. Thank you very much, Madam President. What we were told today in this Council statement is that, for technical reasons, democracy would have to be sacrificed in the context of the European Semester. But what we are discussing here is a political issue, and the question I pose is whether political coordination can exist without democracy.
Where, after all, is the scrutiny of the European Parliament?
Where is the scrutiny of National Parliaments over their major budgetary choices?

But if the issue were technical, as has been stated here, then we are talking about a variable technique. Because we know how the Council substantially eased the conditions for the four countries on the waiting list—France, Italy, Hungary, and Spain—whereas it did not do the same for the countries under rescue programs.
Therefore, what kind of intergovernmentality is this that allows Germany to give opinions about Portugal, but does not allow Portugal to give opinions about Germany?
And, Mr. Commissioner, I kindly ask you only to pay attention to this: no democratic legitimacy issue is resolved by asking Members of Parliament to consult information online.
This is an issue of information exclusion, not a problem of democratic exclusion, which is the issue we are discussing here.

Thank you, Madam President, Commissioner, colleagues. A first word to congratulate the rapporteur from Ardenne on the quality and timeliness of this report, as well as Commissioner Andor for the initiatives announced.
Poverty is indeed a very serious issue that demands effective and swift action to halt the growing trend, which currently affects more than 80 million Europeans.
The severe crisis we are currently experiencing has worsened this situation and made the times we live in dramatic, as Mr. Commissioner Andor stated.
The number of people unemployed continues to rise, and we all know that unemployment is a gateway to poverty and social exclusion.
This situation requires resources, willpower, and considerable effort from the European Commission and the Member States.
If this does not happen, the goal of lifting 20 million European citizens out of poverty during this decade will not be achieved.
Therefore, I warmly welcome the initiative of the Platform against Poverty, which is a very important instrument for ensuring social justice.
But there is no doubt that the best remedies against poverty and social exclusion are job creation and the promotion of economic growth.
Mr. President, access to the internet should today be regarded as a new fundamental right of citizens, and not merely as a commodity for trade.
they depend on essential factors such as freedom of expression, access to and sharing of information, access to and sharing of culture and knowledge, as well as democratic and media pluralism.

And that is why this access cannot be subject to speculative tariffs or contracts that restrict free choice and the ease of switching providers, particularly in the realm of mobile telecommunications, as we also know.
Active citizenship therefore requires that complete and transparent information be made available.
The blocking of internet access is not exclusive to political regimes that fear their people.
It may also occur as a result of economic choices, by offering privileges to potential customers and providing less equitable treatment to customers who are less profitable.
It is precisely for all these reasons that I support the Parliament in clearly standing alongside internet neutrality and in defending its principle of total openness.
Let's face the truth.
We have been taking the medication for three years to get out of the crisis, and the situation is worsening day by day.
Instead of healing, we have created two fatal vicious cycles.
The first among banks and states.
In Europe, it is today easier to discuss the bankruptcy of a state than that of a major bank, yet there remains an expectation that banks finance states and that states rescue their banks.
This spiral must be broken.
The Commission must advance European legislation that protects taxpayers from bank crises.
but it is also vital to protect state debt from attacks by speculators.

And for this purpose, it is urgent and long overdue to prepare coordinated issuances of sovereign debt—whether called eurobonds or stability bonds—and to guarantee them through robust, community-based instruments of a non-intergovernmental nature.
In short, we also need to reconsider the involvement of the European Central Bank.
But the second vicious circle arises from the argument that some states are rich because they are virtuous, while others are poor because they have sinned.
This narrative is politically explosive and technically incorrect.

She ignores the essential fact that a single market and a single currency, built by political choice among unequal partners, necessarily exacerbates competitiveness imbalances between the center and the periphery.
And that is what must be challenged in economic governance.
No, Mr. Rompoy, through sanctions that promote the suppression of wages and incomes, with one country exerting control over another.
Previously, through engagement, Commissioner, both with countries that have surpluses and with those that have deficits, in order to align productivity through investment, education, and science.
The tensions have only produced unemployment, disillusionment, and another form from which we cannot account.

Thank you very much, Madam President, Madam Commissioner. The Integrated Maritime Policy advocates a coordinated approach to the management and governance of oceans, seas, and coasts, within a global and coherent framework of all European policies relating to the sea.

Having a global perspective and an integrated approach entails the interaction of European policies related to the sea and demands improved governance through greater involvement in the European decision-making process.
The success and relevance of this policy have been demonstrated over recent years through various initiatives funded in the form of preparatory actions and pilot projects.
Integrated maritime policy should therefore continue to receive adequate funding over the coming years of the current financial programming period.

In this regard, I advocate for adequate funding up to 2013, not only to consolidate previous projects, but also support the establishment of a stable framework regarding the objectives of this program.

Issues concerning policy, governance, sustainability, and maritime surveillance are, for obvious reasons, particularly relevant to certain regions of the European Union, such as the outermost regions.
The RUPs are regions, except for French Guiana, that are small in size and rely on a limited number of economic activities, in which maritime and fishing activities assume particular importance.
Moreover, the outermost regions contribute to making the European Union possess the world's largest exclusive economic zone.

The RUPs account for more than half of the European Union's exclusive economic zone, approximately 15 out of 25 million square kilometers, and, together with coastal regions, are fundamental to the economic growth of the European Union and its member states.

A program such as the one outlined in the integrated maritime policy should benefit these regions by prioritizing transnational coordination of maritime policies and relevant international cooperation.
Thus, it is necessary to ensure continuous and sufficient funding, not only for the next two years, but also, and above all, for the upcoming financial framework starting in 2014.
Failing to seize the opportunity when discussing integrated maritime policy to emphasize the immense potential that the Atlantic and insular dimensions hold for the European Union.

The Atlantic, as Europe's western maritime frontier, also establishes its connection to North and South America and to Africa, imparting a strategic dimension essential to Europe within the framework of a maritime policy designed with a forward-looking perspective.

And it is with the future in mind that the integrated maritime policy, beyond promoting the coordination of maritime activities, must place at the top of its priorities the potential of the Atlantic in its three-dimensional dimension, based on science and technology.

The exploration of the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, the deep sea, and the seafloor represents a new frontier whose potential in biotechnology and mineral resources is incalculable in value to the knowledge-based economy.
The Union should therefore take advantage of the Azores' privileged location and the excellence of its oceanographic department to establish a Deep Sea Observatory there. Thank you.
Enough of this path.
When the diagnosis is wrong, the medications don't work.
And in the case of the eurozone, excessive remedies have brought it close to death.
The successive miracle solutions that were proposed were likewise discredited by the markets.
the Financial Stabilization Fund, the successive tightening measures of the Stability and Growth Pact, and the revenues from the Troika in low-program countries.
Blind discipline without growth triggered a recessive wave across fully interconnected economies, which thus became contaminated by bankruptcies and unemployment.
And in the hands of the markets—that is, rating agencies and speculators—governments are falling one after another, and solvent countries are turning into insolvent ones.

Amending the treaties now is another step along this disastrous path, with an added risk: we risk destroying what remains of national sovereignty by placing countries in the hands of the European Court of Justice.
No, it is not necessary to amend the treaties.
We must, in this emergency in which we find ourselves, make two decisions.
First of all, a genuine agenda for sustained growth and cohesion must be implemented.
Where is the Agenda 2020?
Where is the restart budget?
Where are those projects, the Project Bonds, for Europe's structural investments?
And, secondly, sovereign debt must be protected.
Of course, eurobonds are necessary, but in the emergency we are currently facing, it is urgent that the European Central Bank, directly or indirectly, defend sovereign debt against speculators.
not in a shamefaced manner, but with a firm, resolute stance, as befits any central bank defending its currency and the economy that supports it.
For this, it is not necessary to amend the treaties; what is needed is political will and European conviction.
We will advance the treaty once we have drawn all the lessons from the present crisis. And there are certainly many lessons to be learned, but this is certainly not the moment. Thank you very much.
Mr. President and Mr. Commissioner Dalli, the first Single Market Forum, an initiative stemming from the Greek report, took place in Kraków
It was rich in content and fruitful in the debate carried out by the Polish Presidency with European, national, regional, and local authorities, as well as civil society.
We have a script outlined in the Commission's communication of April 12, with 12 levers.
It must be incorporated into law, as well as the strategy for the other thirty-eight priorities identified in the single market act.
For a market to exist, trust is necessary.
Now, citizens' and consumers' trust cannot be taken for granted; it must be stimulated and nurtured.

The single market is not merely a buying and selling operation; it involves horizontal policies such as health, social protection and consumer rights, labor law, the environment, and sustainable development.
The Single Market Forum should thus become an emblematic event for the European Union, held annually and attracting a diverse and increasingly participatory audience.
Mr. President, I believe we are at a stage of reassessment regarding these crisis policies, austerity policies that have affected so many countries, as well as the so-called structural reforms.
And it is within this context that I believe we cannot fail to recognize how much social commotion they have generated.
and has generated social confusion not only in countries facing greater difficulties, in Southern countries, not only in the so-called PIGS, but also in other European contexts.
Last week, Portugal experienced its largest general strike ever, involving both the public and private sectors, a strike that took to the streets.

But if we look at what has happened today in the United Kingdom, we see that we are witnessing the largest strike ever, especially considering that people took to the streets much as they did in Portugal.
And we expect a large demonstration in Brussels on Friday.

What this demonstrates, Mr. President, and what the strikes and people taking to the streets show, is that the causes and problems we have been facing are common, and that is precisely why the response is also common.
The crisis of the European Union is affecting all member states, reaching unbelievable proportions.

We have witnessed chaos in financial markets and the growing inability to secure the financing essential for the operations of our small and medium-sized enterprises, even those that are healthy, driving them to despair, bankruptcy, and unemployment.
It is a spiral that fuels the recession and is destroying the economy and society.

No one denies the need for strict budgetary discipline, deep restructuring, and greater economic governance. But these are necessary conditions that, in the short term, are no longer sufficient to reverse the ongoing deterioration within the Union.

And if it is true that policies aimed at employment and growth are needed, without which we will never succeed in controlling sovereign debts, the central issue today is ensuring the financing of our businesses and economies, without which the Union will plunge into crisis.
Therefore, more than ever, more than the revision of the treaties, we today need a strong and credible intervention by the European Central Bank.
Thank you very much, Madam President, Commissioner. We are here today, all of us united around a resolution containing a set of very important demands in terms of intervention.
We are referring to World AIDS Day, and this assessment regarding the adoption and communication of resolution 2009 is fundamental.
It is essential precisely things that have already been mentioned.
We repeat this, unfortunately, because there is still room for repetition, which means there is much yet to be done.
There are many new cases diagnosed each year in Europe and the European Economic Area. A 2010 report mentions more than 25,000 new cases.
If we consider the neighboring countries, we are talking about over one hundred thousand new cases
And if we think there are things that cannot get worse, they can always get worse.
And one of the issues I wish to make very clear here concerns the disinvestment that has occurred in the healthcare sector as a result of the current economic and financial crisis.
and this disinvestment has seriously affected areas that are fundamental to the fight against HIV/AIDS.
That is precisely why, Mr. Commissioner, I believe the Commission has a crucial role to play in urging the Member States to fulfill their obligations in these areas.

A second issue also concerns the increase in discrimination, evidenced by the economic and social crisis, discrimination against workers who have lost their jobs because they are importers of this virus.
That is why prevention, treatment, and care must be equal for everyone, regardless of sex, age, or the region in which they live.
and I hope we can implement these measures with the support of the Commissioner. Thank you very much.
Welcome to this Parliament, Mr. Draghi.
We are living through special and dramatic circumstances, and they require determined and urgent measures.

The spiral between government debt and bank debt must be broken, but at this moment the priority lies in defending the states, ensuring that eurozone countries and the euro itself are not destroyed by credit rating agencies and speculators.
In the report we will approve, Tremosa and Vassal propose ideas to rethink the architecture of the euro zone, create eurobonds and a European treasury, but all of this takes time.
At this moment we are discussing an emergency, and within this context, the only solution lies in the hands of the European Central Bank.
Technically, the European Central Bank knows what needs to be done.
Today, you should not be deterred in your intervention and action by narrow interpretations of your mandate.
Its mandate is to safeguard the euro, not to address inflation.
At a moment like this, there is no doubt. Saving the euro and doing whatever is necessary is precisely what this Parliament and the citizens expect of the European Central Bank.
Good luck and courage, Mr. Draghi.
The European Neighbourhood Policy already explicitly included the positions that the European Parliament has been taking.

And it was with great satisfaction that we saw a substantial portion of the principles and proposals we have been advocating in this House included in the subsequent communications from the Commission and the Council.

I would like to express my gratitude, indeed, for the excellent and ongoing cooperation and coordination we have maintained throughout this period with Vice-President Cathy Ashton and Commissioner Stefan Füle and their respective offices.
right from the start through the improvement achieved in our relationship with neighboring peoples.

By prioritizing relations with the general population and, in particular, with civil society organizations, we will be lending greater objectivity and effectiveness to our neighborhood policy, which is intended to be based exclusively on the universal values we uphold and share.

Thank you, Mr. President. I begin by congratulating my colleague, Ms. Nibler, on her excellent work, and by noting the coincidence that today, on Europe Day, we are debating and tomorrow will adopt measures that respond to the legitimate aspirations of our citizens.

What is at stake in this regulation is imposing limits on mobile phone charges imposed on European consumers when roaming, with its main objective being to eliminate roaming within the European space starting from 2016.
Our citizens will now benefit from a significant reduction in prices regarding calls, SMS messages, and data.
Today, there are operators charging €3 per megabyte, but from July onwards, the maximum they may charge will be 70 cents per megabyte, decreasing to 20 cents per megabyte in 2014.
Regarding incoming calls, the maximum price that may be charged will be 8 cents this year and 5 cents in 2014.
In July 2012, a family with two mobile phone users will save, as the Commissioner said, 200 euros on their bill.
Second benefit: higher quality of information provided to consumers.
Starting from summer, operators will be required to inform consumers of the prices charged when they travel outside the European Union as well.
Until now, the obligation applied only within the European space.
Third advantage: the possibility of more operators entering the market, meaning increased competition that will encourage better services and lower prices.

Finally, in the near future, the consumer may, if desired, have two contracts—one for services within the national territory and another for services abroad—while keeping the same mobile number and receiving only a single bill.
The consumer, our citizen, will likewise be given the opportunity to contract with any European operator offering them the best price.
