Mr. President, Minister Vaman, the first thing I would like to state is that it is regrettable that at a debate of this importance, the Vice-President of the Commission and the High Representative, Ms. Ashton, are not present.
In any case, we are pleased to work once again with Minister Vaman, whose statement here fully reflects my own views.

First of all, I would like to offer a word of comfort and solidarity to the people of Guinea, primarily the people of Guinea-Bissau, a people systematically massacred by democratic instability, political instability, and widespread insecurity.
Not only is he surrounded by poverty, but he has also been systematically affected by these afflictions.

I would like to say, on the other hand, that the PPE, during its presidency, has followed this issue closely from the outset, and it is regrettable that the European Parliament only managed to hold a debate on this matter after a month.
This relates to the invisibility of the Guinean people and the Guinean issue.

And this invisibility is due solely to the fact that, unlike other matters—also very serious—no major economic interests are at stake here that would prompt European, American, and global powers to debate the situation of the Guinean people.

But I must say, if an external reason is needed, if poverty and political instability alone are not sufficient to prompt us to take global humanitarian and political action, then there is such a reason.
unless the situation regarding drug trafficking spreading in Guinea were a reason for us to commit ourselves to this effort.
And therefore, I ask the European Union to be stronger.

There is no doubt that Resolution 2048, there is no doubt that the decisions of the European Union, the Community of Portuguese-Speaking Countries, and the African Union, but perhaps no longer the Community of West African States, are moving in the right direction.
But resolutions are not enough; actions are required.
We must be firmer, we must be more effective with the states that have the capacity to intervene in Guinea-Bissau.
This is our obligation.

And I believe that the European Union is currently responding adequately in terms of principles, but is not responding as it should in terms of practical instruments and on-the-ground implementation, and it is this that I request of the Council and I request of the Commission.

Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, globalization has brought about an disproportionate growth of the financial sector, whose transactions today, as is well known, are seventy times greater than global wealth.

Opacity, extremely frequent transactions, high remunerations, and excessive risk have not only come to characterize the world we live in today, but have also become fundamental causes of the crisis we are experiencing.
However, this crisis has held ordinary citizens accountable and demanded far greater efforts from them than from the sector that caused it.
Thus, today, this moment is important.

It is important, and personally, I would like to achieve a situation in which the tax is generalized across all countries of the European Union and subsequently extended worldwide, through the active participation of the Commission and the European Union as a whole within the G20 context.
I would like it to encompass or include all products without exception.
I would like it to serve as a basis for the necessary strengthening of the European Union's own resources.
In the short term, compromises must be accepted, but we must not lose sight of the direction we must follow.
And this can only be achieved through a clear identification of these objectives by this Parliament, by the Commission, and with a strong spirit of compromise also from the Council.

This is what European citizens expect from us today, and here and now, I can only thank not only Ani Podimata, who was tireless throughout this process, but also everyone who managed to overcome their differences to reach a common position.

Thank you, Mr. President. Thank you, colleague Podimata. Today we are discussing here for the first time the tax on financial transactions, and its approval would be the first signal that speculation will not go entirely unpunished in the crisis we are experiencing.
This House and the European institutions must be accountable to people, not to financial markets.
For decades, financial markets' lobbies managed to govern the world without opposition and without any problems.
It was obvious to us that during 2008 and 2012 the crisis of the speculative financial market lay at the root of most of the problems we experienced.

And yet, these speculative financial markets managed not only to ensure that nothing was done, but also to effect a direct transfer from the pockets of citizens straight into these very same speculative markets.

We have been fighting for this for many years, Mr. President, but it was only when peoples throughout Europe began to say enough was finally some initiative taken by the institutions governing the European Union.
And therefore, this is the answer we must give to citizens. It is a first signal we must send.
The citizens of Europe, who have paid everything, deserve not to be disrespected.
And therefore, this proposal is not complete, not perfect, and will not solve the entire problem of financial speculation, but it is an important signal.
We have been, no, European leaders have been apologizing to financial markets
They should apologize to the people they have affected.
And, to conclude, Mr. President, quoting a politician who is now famous, it is of these markets that we are speaking, of these markets that govern us without ever having been elected.

I sincerely hope that governments send a signal of trust to people, and I sincerely hope that, with this approval and with the positive vote of my colleagues, we can begin to speak of justice and solidarity in Europe.
Colleagues, muster your courage—we will push this project through.
Thank you, Mr. President. The financial transaction tax should serve as a means of controlling and discouraging high-risk speculative activities in the financial sector.
It aims to reduce speculation and promote healthy competition, the foundation of the single market, and also generate revenue.
The Podimata report sought to align the European Commission's initial proposal with the well-known UK stamp duty. In this way, we made the proposal more appealing to hesitant member states.
It is expected that the Council will also understand the message.

Whereas in the Commission's proposal the tax applied to transactions conducted by financial institutions located within the territory of the European Union, it is now intended that transactions involving European assets, even when carried out by non-Europeans, should also be taxed.
This measure will broaden the tax base and reduce revenue losses.
It is expected that tax revenues will be regarded as own resources of the Union or the States, thereby reducing the taxes of the member states, a matter still to be decided.
I conclude, Mr. President, by congratulating the rapporteur on the quality of her work and I hope that this symbolic text...
Thank you, Madam President. This past weekend, we had yet another request to rescue the private banking sector.

They keep repeating the same argument, which is to prevent contagion to the eurozone. And I ask myself whether we have learned nothing. Greece, Portugal, Ireland, and now Spain—and yet the contagion continues.
But what root of contagion containment is this that is destroying us?

This policy isn't working—we should already know that. It's the same policymakers who claim there's no money for culture, education, healthcare, and public goods, yet when banks request funds from the markets, the money appears almost miraculously.
This economic policy is not a remedy; it is a deadly poison for growth, employment, the European economy, and the European social model.
Markets dictate, and governments obey, threatening the people. And that is precisely what governments are doing to Greece at this moment, during an election period.
I sincerely hope that, on Sunday, the Greek people will not be intimidated and will instead give us a lesson, because this recipe, Madam President, is a recipe for disaster. Thank you very much.

Thank you very much, Mr. President. Colleagues, unfortunately, the proposals we have discussed here today confirm the prohibition of crisis prevention policies in the eurozone and further entrench the logic of the Fiscal Treaty.

There are some statements of intent, but they are very timid. I reiterate, for example, the script for a day when it might become possible to begin considering ways to define a potential legislation for the hypothetical introduction of eurobonds.
The problem is that this day will only come when all the problems in the eurozone have been resolved—and it will take a very long time, indeed, to arrive.
We support certain aspects of this proposal, but at this pace, it will all be far too little and far too late.
Placing a country under legal protection entails maintaining the rates and amounts, as well as prioritizing new loans. In other words, it requires...
Member states that honor their debt are deprived of all and every instrument that might enable them to grow and secure the resources to do so.

Let us be frank with one another. The only legal protection provided in these proposals—and the only one that is foreseen—is for creators, but even that will not be effective for them, as it is not effective for creators either.

As we have seen in Greece, Ireland, Portugal, and now Spain, this means condemning countries to recession, unemployment, and insolvency. And, Mr. President, I conclude by saying that the worsening of these situations and their spread to other economies brings us close to a point of no return.
Faced with the complete failure of austerity, the European Commission or the German Government offers us the same policy, but in overdose. It is neoliberal fanaticism that is killing the euro.
Thank you very much, Mr. President. Given the extremely limited time we have, I simply wish to say at this moment to the Commission that it can count, as it has always been able to count, on this...
Parliament in effective work for the solidity of the eurozone and Europe, but this is only achieved when initiatives focusing on discipline are accompanied by

We have managed to establish the conditions for a balanced sharing of benefits and sacrifices among the different Member States and among citizens, and a hope—a hope—that we will emerge from the crisis we are currently experiencing.
For this hope to be realized, other initiatives are needed, and it is these that this Parliament is seeking to provide—one initiative focused on investment, growth, and oversight.
of sovereign debt, which has been the Achilles' heel of those evident weaknesses reflecting the fragility of the economic systems of some of the member countries.
Mr. Commissioner, you can count on us, Council, Madam President, you can count on us, but the agenda must be a comprehensive one; it cannot be an agenda that makes.
the subject or condition upon which everything else depends, or what makes discipline the alpha and omega of our European proposals.

Our citizens expect this of us; they can count on the Parliament, and I would like to thank once again my colleagues, the rapporteurs, Mr. Gozés, who is the other rapporteur, and all the other colleagues who, whether as shadow rapporteurs or merely active contributors to the development of this project, have been able to make a meaningful contribution.
for a more balanced, more solid Parliament project, one that I hope will deserve the respect, the capacity for dialogue, and the spirit of cooperation and compromise it has shown so far.
Successful. Many thanks, Mr. President. Many thanks, Madam President. Many thanks, Commissioner.

Thank you very much, Madam President. In addition to thanking all those who contributed to this outcome, I would like to request that the final vote be postponed so that we may immediately begin negotiations at the working level.
Today, we face three agendas. The growth agenda, the agenda concerning bank management, and the agenda concerning sovereign debt management.
And addressing the European Semester implies incorporating these three dimensions.
On the issue of growth, I believe it is important at this moment to emphasize that the Commission's recommendations cannot apply solely to...
countries with deficits. They must also apply to countries with surpluses. And if some have to suppress their domestic demand, others must release theirs.

I also believe that, at the level of growth, it is essential that the Commission oversee the recommendations from the so-called Troika, because when a country enters recession, it cannot continue to crush public investment and public spending as if nothing had happened.
The trilogues must have their draft reviewed and adjusted according to the outcomes. And I would like to tell you, Mr. Commissioner, that today we have approved here in Parliament
Parliament, the need to introduce an instrument for growth and countercyclical activity amounting to approximately 1% of the EU's GDP over a 10-year period.
A second fundamental dimension is what is now called the Banking Union. We requested this in Parliament two years ago, in a June 2010 report. To date, it remains unfulfilled.

A third dimension is the dimension of sovereign debt. Without it, there can be no stability in Europe. And I would also like to say that today we approved the TUPAC here, but within the TUPAC, beyond austerity,

some other dimension that has been missing, and that is the immediate establishment of a redemption fund, using German terminology, so that sovereign debt exceeding sixty percent can be managed collectively.

This is a legislative text, and we await the Commission's strong support for this initiative by the Parliament, so that we may contain the completely threatening spiral currently undermining the European Union. Thank you very much.

Thank you very much, Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues. Now that we intend to discuss a growth pact, it is essential not to forget citizenship and consumers, ultimately the human and social dimension.

which gives it substance and was the subject of one of the three reports on the Single Market Act from 2001. Because any growth pact must take into account the social sacrifices and benefits associated with it.

As important as an internal market test will be conducting a social impact assessment of all measures taken to achieve the single market. A test, yes, an examination that enables us to measure the progress made in the benefit of European Union citizens.
Since 2008, Europe has already lost more than 6 million jobs.
In my country, Portugal, the economy is experiencing a contraction unprecedented in history, and unemployment among men, women, and young people is reaching record highs.
According to forecasts by the Bank of Portugal, the situation will worsen over the next two years.
It is within this context that we support and welcome the initiative of the new employment package, and we congratulate Commissioner Andor.
Emphasizing once again and always the fundamental premise of the need for economic growth, Member States will truly have to focus their efforts on job creation.

The means to achieve this should include hiring subsidies that generate new jobs, reduction of the tax burden on labor, support for the creation of new businesses and entrepreneurship, as well as assistance to small and medium-sized enterprises that preserve existing jobs.

It is essential that national employment policies keep in mind that the areas with the greatest potential for job creation are health, information and communication technologies, and the green economy.
As stated in the communication, it is urgent to narrow the gap between the qualifications provided by education and the competences demanded by the labor market.
On the other hand, one of the solutions to combating unemployment lies in the mobility of workers.
It is also crucial that the use of structural funds for job creation and the practical importance of the Euros portal as the primary instrument for job recruitment and job offers across Europe.
Finally, social governance must be a top priority, as our citizens are eagerly awaiting concrete results. Thank you very much.

Thank you very much, Madam President, Commissioner. It is true that six months have already passed since this scandal involving GDP, and what is most incredible is that for ten years a private company managed to...
operate in the market and have introduced silicone implants intended for use in the construction and manufacture of mattresses, which were subsequently used in women across 71 countries.
This affected some hundreds of thousands of women, as already mentioned; it is estimated to be approximately 500,000.

Now, we are speaking of estimates because there were failures—and serious failures—in this process. We know that what occurred with this company was a criminal case, a fraud, a matter for court—but we also know...

There is here a fraud that must be addressed by the courts, and the courts will deal with it. But there is a legal flaw, a legal gap, and this is what we must address. We have responsibilities in this matter.

For 10 years we were unable to identify what the criminals were able to identify. And therefore, there was a failure in terms of control, traceability, file management, and record-keeping. Everything failed. But these individuals from this company managed to implement what we were unable to control.

This process is not merely a matter of lack of information, which is indeed very important—the lack of information on all matters concerning breast implants—but also the humiliation of thousands of women, and particularly the humiliation experienced by those women who were forced to undergo tests to determine whether they had received the correct implants or not.

We are talking about a matter of public health, and we cannot afford to be lax regarding public health. We regulate so many unnecessary aspects; we even regulate the authenticity of replacement parts in automobiles—just recently, we adopted a directive on this matter.

We must regulate what is essential to people's lives. The revision of the Medical Devices Directive is good news. It was the best news that we wouldn't have to do it. But now that we are going to do it, let us apply it with...

conditions, with traceability, transparency, control, and sanctions, because, unfortunately, even when sanctions are applied, they will not be applied to the women who have suffered from this issue, and states must assume full responsibility.
Mr. President, I would like to clearly state here that for us, the Council scheduled for the end of June is essential, and what is crucial is that we seek a European solution to this crisis.
From our perspective, the problem so far has been that we have been addressing each emerging issue in isolation and individually.

The time has come for us to have a comprehensive solution for Europe, one that, as President Dole clearly emphasized here, involves greater integration, enhanced cooperation, closer alignment of European policies, and a renewed appreciation of the community method.
It is essential that alongside stability there be growth, but that we not abandon our concerns for stability.
We therefore count on a comprehensive European response, rather than a fragmented response for each State or each region facing difficulties.
Productivity issues and increased production possibilities led to considerable increases in milk output, creating critical situations, surpluses, and low prices in the milk and dairy products market.

In many regions with high productive potential, these surpluses were sold at discount prices on the markets of other countries, further increasing downward pressure on the prices paid to producers in those countries.

This occurred, for example, with French milk sold in France at twice the price it was sold in Galicia. And producers, facing a sharp rise in the cost of raw materials and production factors, now find their ability to withstand low prices greatly diminished.

This is a poor prognosis for numerous enterprises and many productive regions of Europe, and it also appears to be a clear indication of what the future may hold in the absence of quotas and without adequate supply regulation instruments.

It therefore demands a thorough audit of the territorial impacts on thousands of farms across the European Union resulting from the abolition of these instruments currently in place to regulate supply.
Thank you, Mr. President. I begin by congratulating the rapporteur colleague on this report.
This is a matter of utmost importance for Europe.
at a time when we are experiencing a severe economic, financial, and social crisis, protecting intellectual property rights by combating counterfeit products is essential for the European economy.
This protection should strengthen research performance and promote innovation. It should ensure scientific and technological knowledge, contributing to the development of new products and services.
All of this in the sense of generating growth and creating quality jobs.

Unfortunately, in recent years we have witnessed a significant increase in the violation of intellectual property rights in Europe, resulting in annual losses of 250 billion euros for companies, as already mentioned here, and the destruction of 185,000 jobs across the European region.

However, beyond these negative effects on the European economy, the violation of these rights also poses a serious threat to the health and safety of European consumers, as many counterfeit products—such as foodstuffs, cosmetics, pharmaceuticals, and others—
etc., are produced in unregulated environments.
Therefore, the customs authorities of the European Union play a crucial role in preventing counterfeit products from entering the internal market.
With the revision of the present regulation, the aim is to strengthen oversight of the implementation of the legislation in question, reinforce the legitimacy of its intervention, and increase the...
legal clarity, by adapting the provisions of the Regulation to new realities and developments, thus contributing to better protection of the European economy and consumers.
Intellectual property rights are therefore a crucial priority for achieving the objectives of the 2020 Strategy, aiming for smart, sustainable, and inclusive growth. Thank you very much.

Thank you, Mr. President. Indeed, the election of the new Egyptian president in the post-Mubarak era is good news. However, we must not deceive ourselves, and these presidential elections in no way conclude the transition process currently taking place in Egypt.

It has been rightly pointed out that dissolving Parliament just days before the presidential elections brings several risks to the forefront. And in this case, the power concentrated in the Superior Council of...

The Armed Forces were the least exaggerated element. They possess legislative power, have reduced the President's authority, and furthermore hold in their hands the power to determine changes to the Constitution.

Now, all this raises certain concerns—and should raise concerns in us—because the Egyptians risk becoming hostages to military power and a return to the past, lacking a fundamental element: a democratically elected parliament.
It is not for us to bring about change in Egypt; it is up to the Egyptians.
but I believe that we Europeans will have to stand alongside all changes that aim to restore democratic legitimacy and a democratically elected Parliament. Many thanks.

Thank you very much, Madam President, esteemed colleagues, Madam Commissioner. I begin by congratulating my fellow rapporteur, Lara Come, on the excellent work she has carried out in this report, which is of vital importance for Europe's future.
In a world and society undergoing rapid transformation, Europe needs a comprehensive, integrated, effective, and up-to-date standardization system.
A system that ensures the proper functioning of the internal market, improves consumer protection and their quality of life, and also safeguards the environment, is essential.

as well as the importance of greater innovation and social inclusion, and the interaction and communication among networks and systems, particularly in the domain, as already emphasized here, of new information and communication technologies.
As the professor Monti stated in his report, a new strategy for the single market makes European standardization essential for the governance of the internal market, emphasized.
the need to review the European standardization process, maintaining the advantages of the current system and achieving a fair balance between the European and national dimensions.

In this sense, the report we are now discussing follows and supports these objectives, which are correct and appropriate. Ending the sluggishness and fragmentation that exist, which cause companies to incur costs

higher transaction costs and higher unit costs, strengthen the participation of SMEs in standardization activities, particularly at the national level, as well as facilitate their access to these standards, strengthen also

On the other hand, the involvement of stakeholders representing societal interests, such as consumers, environmentalists, workers, and persons with disabilities, should be maintained, along with financial support for European organizations established to represent public interests.

I conclude by stating that the approval of this report will contribute to the revitalization of the European project, to European innovation, and to strengthening and competitiveness of our enterprises. Thank you very much.
Thank you very much, Mr. President, Madam Commissioner. The future common fisheries policy must give priority to its environmental, economic, and social dimensions.
in a synergistic and non-hierarchical manner, in support of sustainable development and in line with the objectives of smart, sustainable, and inclusive growth set forth in the EU 2020 Strategy.
As rapporteur for the Regi opinion, I would like to highlight the particular circumstances of certain regions, such as the outermost regions, whose economies are heavily dependent on the sector...

In these regions, the reform of the common fisheries policy should aim to ensure the future survival and prosperity of their small-scale and artisanal fishing fleets, thereby safeguarding and securing the employment dependent upon them.

I agree with the European Commission's position regarding the need to adjust this policy and implement specific measures that take into account the differing realities of the fishing and aquaculture sectors across various coastal regions.

I argue that this policy should focus on regionalization as a governance approach, by strengthening the participation of various stakeholders, so that, in the future, responses can be more appropriately tailored to the requirements and sensitivities of each maritime basin.
We are reaching the decisive moments in which we will shape the new fisheries policy in Europe, and we cannot afford to fail.

The need and urgency to achieve sustainable balances in our solar resources, as well as in the revitalization of our coastal fishing communities and the promotion of their socio-economic level, in the modernization of the sector, and in the provision of high-quality food, demand courage and bold measures like never before.
We therefore have to make choices and prioritize objectives.

I proposed protection for the vulnerability of my region by suggesting that the exploitation of its resources be based on scientific data, that our coastal communities be given priority in their exploitation, and that external access be restricted beyond 100 miles to existing fishing banks.
I proposed safeguarding the fishing capacity of our important fishing communities, rejecting the proposals for transferable fishing quotas.

I proposed that our communities have greater involvement in managing their own affairs, particularly in fisheries, through the establishment of a dedicated Regional Advisory Council better suited to the needs of the outermost regions. I hope that in this way we can achieve a better future.

Given what we know about the implementation of the third package and considering that part of this issue has already been discussed today, regarding the delay in transposing its directives, the most recent information available to me indicates that...

18 Member States have been notified; this information is likely already outdated due to lack of transposition. The question I pose to you is how you view the possibility of, in the legislation being prepared—particularly regarding networks—
trans-European, if it includes a device, an incentive that conditions eligibility for the submission of PCIs, projects of common interest.

condition the fulfillment by Member States of their obligations arising from the third package, or conversely, consider this a cumbersome mechanism that should be left solely to the regular competition mechanisms, Article 101,
and their substitutes, all intervention in this matter. Many thanks.
by the Commission and the tolerance, the spirit of tolerance that has existed in this matter. But the issue is whether, given that legislation is being prepared that may...
introduce an additional and much stronger conditionality, do you consider this conditionality to be an abusive instrument, or do you think such conditionality would be acceptable?
I confess that I share much of the concern initially raised here regarding the democratic legitimacy and democratic oversight related to this proposal from the European Union.
Banking Union and Banking Supervision, because an essential issue concerning the role of Parliament—the only institution democratically elected directly by citizens—remains unclear and unresolved.

But there is another concern I also have, one that goes deeper and relates to the following: monetary policy instruments are controlled by the European Union. Monetary policy falls under the responsibility of the European Union.

Bank supervision also becomes the responsibility of the European Union. Yet, if things go wrong and banks fail, the responsibility for covering that failure remains with the taxpayers of the national states, remaining a national responsibility.

This is not a matter of relative importance; it is a substantive issue that remains unresolved. And finally, I would simply like to point out that the fact that we continue to tie monetary policy to what is, in our current context, a secondary concern—namely inflation—rather than to employment creation and growth, as it should be, also remains unresolved. Thank you very much.

Thank you very much, Mr. President. European leaders continue to emphasize budgetary discipline, and I ask: budgetary discipline—then let's put it into practice. Because what exactly are we talking about when we refer to budgetary discipline?

In my understanding, budgetary discipline means honoring contracts with pensioners and not abandoning the State's commitments midway. In my understanding, budgetary discipline means respecting the agreements of those who work and not inventing extraordinary taxes or cutting subsidies.

The budget must respect commitments in health, education, and culture, and must not close services or halt and cancel projects midway. Discipline means restoring banks to order, rather than taking money from taxpayers and handing it to speculators in order to save banks.
This is the fiscal discipline I would like to see implemented in the European Union, rather than continuing this celebration of the markets.

If an example is needed, the budget currently being discussed in Portugal under the scrutiny of the Troika is a perfect example. It violates every commitment any State should have toward its citizens in order not to breach the agreements made behind closed doors that are bringing Europe and its nations together.

Thank you very much, Mr. President, Mr. President of the Council, Mr. President of the Commission. First of all, I would naturally like to congratulate you on the outcomes of the Council, although it seems somewhat delayed in timing, with the final decision arriving somewhat late.
The truth is that there is progress.
Now, precisely because I recognize myself in everything said by my colleague Cassolides, I would like to ask just one question of the Council President. The countries under assistance...
Countries facing difficulties have struggled extensively to ensure that banking debts can be applied retroactively in their separation from state debt.
This apparently was not accepted.
But yesterday we heard statements from the highest German authorities and the highest French authorities indicating that they would treat Ireland in a special manner.

What I am asking is whether the Council has approved any decision on treatment that discriminates against Ireland, different from that reserved for countries such as Portugal, Greece, Spain, and others.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, Commissioner, it is a privilege to present the report on the 20 concerns of citizens and businesses.
Regarding the functioning of the single market, precisely in this debate with Commissioner Barnier, marking the twentieth anniversary of this market.

A first word of thanks to colleagues from all political groups, and in particular to the rapporteurs and shadow rapporteurs, for your very constructive contributions and your highly positive attitude.
It was a pleasure working with everyone, and the report greatly benefited from our combined efforts.

We are well aware and take pride in the fact that, over these 20 years, the single market has been the driving force behind major economic and employment opportunities across Europe, transforming the lives of European citizens and European businesses.
Traveling, residing, and working in the area of the Union are freedoms available to all citizens of the 27 member states.
For European consumers, the single market means greater choice and lower prices.
For our businesses, the single market means greater business opportunities, with access to 500 million consumers, which translates into more economic growth and more job creation.
To illustrate the progress the single market has achieved in Europe since its creation in 1992 until the onset of the crisis,
2008, one need only look at the 2.7 million jobs created and the additional 2.13% GDP growth.
Today, making or receiving a phone call while traveling in Europe is 13% cheaper than it was in 2005.
The freedom of movement of people finds its most eloquent expression in the Erasmus programme.
Over two and a half million students have benefited from this European Program for Educational Exchange and Mobility.
Over the past five years, companies have also benefited from a 25% reduction in administrative positions due to the legislation of the single market.
But one question arises. Is everything going well in the single market?
No, not everything works well.
The economic and financial crisis affecting us has had negative repercussions on the functioning of this market.

Our citizens, in some countries living through the hardship of unemployment and facing austerity measures, have come to view Europe with skepticism and hold a negative perception of the internal market.
People still do not know or fully understand their rights, nor do they know where to find information or assistance.
On the other hand, many Member States unjustifiably delay the transposition of directives and European legislation concerning the single market, thereby undermining its full potential.

In certain areas of citizens' everyday lives, things as simple as, for example, the reimbursement of medical expenses following a temporary stay in another Member State, or a student from the programme opening a bank account...

Erasmus, changing energy suppliers, transferring pension payments from one member state to another, recognition of qualifications, and registering a vehicle in another member state remain very complex, difficult, or costly actions.
It is important to address these issues.
That was my concern while serving as rapporteur.
Therefore, the message conveyed in the report is very clear. Greater political will is needed—much greater political will—from the Member States and European institutions.

Council, above all the Council and the Commission, to govern better and jointly. It is necessary to regain the trust of citizens and businesses, and it is essential to facilitate the exercise of their rights.

Finally, I take this opportunity to greet the European Commission, represented by Commissioner Barnier, for presenting the Action Plan for the Single Market 2.0—12 priority actions for renewed growth, which I am certain marks a new stage in our future. Thank you very much.
Madam President, Commissioner Barnier, dear colleague Regina Bastos, my congratulations on your already highly successful efforts.
When Jacques Delors chaired the European Commission, the Single Market of the Union was established in 1992.
Delors then summarized the three fundamental principles of the single market: competition that stimulates, cooperation that strengthens, and solidarity that unites.
Twenty years later, the economic and financial crisis reignited the threat of national protectionism, making it clear that the internal market is not a completed project.

And the celebration of these 20 years must be seized as an opportunity for relaunching the single market, with the aim of placing the market at the service of the citizen, rather than the citizen at the service of the market.
To this end, it is necessary to establish priorities, and I would like to discuss one priority in particular.
A common policy is essential to produce and distribute energy at affordable prices for consumers, with a high level of energy efficiency.
through the European energy market, served by cross-border infrastructure, with the possibility of leveraging community funding to support investment that represents added European value.
In energy as well, Madam President, it will dominate the common market.
one of the greatest achievements of the European Union, which undoubtedly represents one of the strongest pillars in the development of the Union, its member states, and its regions.
However, for it to be effective and fair, its policies must emphasize equality of opportunity for citizens and all regions, promoting the Union's internal cohesion.

In this context, it is essential to recognize that we can only achieve greater development in our most remote regions, such as the outermost regions, through their deeper integration into the single market.

Regions such as the Azores need to continue enhancing their competitiveness by improving their competitive conditions. But we will never achieve this unless we resolve our accessibility issues in the transport sector.
We can achieve this through specific instruments that better address the problems raised by our detachment.
We therefore argue that the Commission should consider a specific POSEI Transport program as an essential instrument for integrating this type of region into the internal market.
Thank you very much, Madam President. I begin by saying thank you very much for this excellent debate.
It was a very constructive debate, and one that demonstrated that the single market is indeed a major achievement of the European project.

I welcome the presence of Minister Mavroianis and his participation in this debate. I thank all my colleagues for their constructive approach, and to those who have adopted a more skeptical stance—understandable as it may be.

and to say thank you very much to Commissioner Barnier for the commitment and enthusiasm he brings to the discussion of these issues, these challenges facing the internal market, and the political will to address them.
We still have much to answer to citizens.
Citizens are dissatisfied, disillusioned, and expect more from us, expect more from the Council, expect more from the Commission, expect more from the Member States.
This review is very useful; this review of the past 20 years is important. But, as many have said here, it is crucial to open the door to a look toward the future.
And the forward-looking perspective encompasses many issues raised here and numerous measures already announced by the European Commission.
The digital single market, citizens' mobility, the reform of the EURES portal, and the European energy market are examples.
eloquent testimony that we still have an important journey ahead in the future, and that this journey is of great importance and vital in addressing this era of crisis we are living through.
Citizens are suffering, citizens expect much from politicians and from the responses that politicians can provide to the adverse conditions they are enduring.
Therefore, it is important that the single market be one of the responses to the major challenge of competitiveness, employment, and economic growth.
I am certain that all of us, following this celebration of our 20th anniversary, are more motivated than ever to provide the answers our citizens need. Thank you very much.

The Gozés report, together with the report we will prepare on the annual growth prospects of the Union at the end of this year and which will precede the Spring Council, are two essential components of the so-called European Semester.
This report addresses the recommendations made to each of the member countries by the Council on the Commission's proposal. And these recommendations, as just mentioned, are framed within...
the SIXPEC and the future TUPEC, an exceptionally powerful body that strengthens European control over the economic and budgetary policies of member countries.

The balance between this control and national autonomy and legitimacy is difficult to achieve and has declined to a level of imposition, particularly in terms of discipline, and ultimately may even trigger a serious package of sanctions.

Regarding the Tupac, I would like briefly to comment that it is important that the Council, which is currently absent here, grant its negotiators a negotiating mandate consistent with the ambition of swiftly concluding this package, this Tupac.

But more European coordination, returning to this theme, means less control by countries over their own destiny, which requires a subtle balance, yet also increased demands regarding the quality of the recommendations made to countries across at least three or four dimensions.

First, it is essential to ensure coherence between medium- and long-term objectives, which include growth, employment, and real convergence, and short-term recommendations, which focus primarily on discipline and nominal convergence. Second, there must be coherence

with the conclusions that must be drawn from the analysis of macroeconomic imbalances, particularly regarding the asymmetric impact of common policies and negative externalities, as well as the spillover effects of certain national policies on other countries.

Thirdly, we must agree that there is a minimum threshold of social, economic, and labor rights that must not be exceeded by these recommendations. And lastly, it is important that the quality of the recommendations be assessed according to the results achieved, which is far from being the case thus far.

I conclude now, Mr. President. I would simply like to thank Mr. Gozés for his spirit of collaboration and to hope that he takes yet another step in this direction, thereby broadening the consensus around his report, and that this spirit will also remain in relation to the next.
It is true, and has already been mentioned here many times, that there are many things we do not know about shale gas extraction.
but there are also many that we already know and that we ought not to forget.
For example, we know that each shale gas extraction operation uses more than 19 million liters of fresh water, enough to supply the annual needs of one thousand European citizens.

We also know that each shale gas extraction, in each operation, pumps approximately 300 tons—excuse me, up to 300 tons—of chemicals into the ground, and about 80% of these remain underground and never leave.

We also know that each shale gas extraction releases radioactive elements and heavy metals into the subsurface, which, together with the evaporation of toxic residues resulting from the process, constitute a real threat to public health.
This poses a risk of contamination of drinking water resources, and it is a real and imminent contamination.

Knowing this, we understand that the extraction of shale gas is also a response that appears easy, focusing solely on the immediate, on industry and profit, while ignoring populations, the future, and the environment.

But this is also the Europe that knows, in this House, that water is an essential, vital, and important resource, that understands the need to protect citizens' health and must continue to do so, that seeks agreements to prevent emissions and to promote energy efficiency, because we know that environmental standards are important, as are standards for health.
Therefore, when one does not know everything, being prudent is the least one can do.
And they may even wish to do so, but let them not do it in our name or in the name of the citizens we represent here.
Thank you very much, Mr. President. Colleagues, indeed, the past years have shown us how the Economic and Monetary Union failed to withstand the crisis.
They also showed us that we had a European institutional architecture full of holes, in particular the institutional architecture of the euro.
And that is why the report presented here and the proposal put forward are far more ambitious than what is offered by the governments.

But also, as the rapporteur stated, we are operating in a realm of political choices, which is why I would like to clarify the reasons why we have certain objections and why we will not be able to support this report.
The first one concerns issues related to conditions.

What we see and continue to maintain is a narrative in which solidarity among different countries can only exist once everything has been fulfilled regarding the stability and growth program, regarding the intergovernmental agreement, the pact for...
euros plus a set of measures that impose nothing other than massive doses of austerity in the peripheries, as well as a set of recessionary measures, which, as we have already seen, are not working.
A second reason relates to the Banking Union.

The rapporteur presents the banking supervision mechanism very well, but it is so essential that we cannot avoid addressing the fundamental issues. And the fundamental issues are being overlooked to the extent that we continue to add the...

agglomerated into national systems, meaning that we are all together in banking policy, we are all together in banking supervision, but we are all separated when something goes wrong, and thus taxpayers will continue to pay.
And a third point concerns the issue of common debt and the issuance of eurobonds.
Here, once again, the possibility of issuing common debt is considered—but only when it is already too late.

because we need the debt as it is now, not only when deficits and excessive debts have been corrected, since with the policies we are currently pursuing, they will never be corrected, but on the contrary, will be increasingly exacerbated, to the despair of entire populations.

Therefore, Mr. President, I wish to conclude by saying that, despite all efforts, I believe we have here a very clear example that together we might still rise, but divided we will surely fall. It was precisely for this reason that it was so important to go even further.
There is always room for improvement, but for Portugal, the support from the European Union in recent years has been fundamental.
Portugal has transformed from a country whose competitiveness was based on cheap labor into a country with highly skilled, highly sophisticated labor.
But when the country exports, it is necessary to have conditions for where?

And that is why it is important that some countries with surpluses open their markets to the requalified exports of countries that started from a specialization profile that was absolutely unsustainable.

A final note to emphasize that it is not acceptable for immigration to be the solution, that requalified labor, especially where support from the European Union has been provided, should not now be lost through a massive export of the most qualified individuals to countries that have the capacity to receive them.
This is dramatic and completely undermines our future. Thank you very much.

Thank you, Mr. President. Small-scale fishing deserves special protection under the common fisheries policy, not only because it promotes socio-economic cohesion and ensures the livelihood of many families, but also because it serves as a factor in anchoring these communities along coastal areas.
Their economic and social sustainability must be a goal, ensuring them a differentiated fishing regime that grants priority access to resources
allows for local management and safeguards the fishing rights enjoyed by fishing professionals from these communities against transferable fishing rights.
It is equally important, for its sustainability, to take into account the access regime to waters, particularly in waters or regions with biogeographical basins.
coastlines with a limited continental shelf and scarce resources concentrated in submarine seamounts and fishing banks that must be granted protected access.

In a context of liberalization and crisis weighing on vulnerable regions, it must likewise be ensured that products originating from international trade meet the same requirements as those imported by the Union.
Mr. President, Mr. President of the Board of Directors, Commissioner, I shall be very brief, as I believe the issue at hand is essentially political in nature.
a financial issue. And the question that arises is the following: whether we want the European Union to make a difference in the context of the crisis, or whether we want to worsen the crisis.

Most European countries are today making an enormous effort, from the standpoint of their public finances, in order to overcome the crisis in a competitive manner, in a way that can position their countries...
The European Union, on a global scale, is highly competitive and capable of competition; however, it is obvious that without investment, there can be no growth and this crisis cannot be overcome.
If the European Union insists on these drastic cuts, particularly in the cohesion policy and the common agricultural policy, what this will mean is that the countries which are making an enormous...
effort, they will not succeed in growing, and this plan we have accepted in all countries for the European Union will fail. Sound accounts with prospects for growth and competitiveness.

The only and major instrument for this is precisely this multiannual financial framework. And therefore, if we want the European Union to succeed and for this policy to become an exemplary model for other countries, we must commit to the budget for the financial perspectives.
